text: Douglas Keszler is an American chemist known for his work in material and inorganic chemistry at Oregon State University, where he became a distinguished professor in 2006. He received his Ph.D. from Northwestern University in 1984 under James A. Ibers and was a postdoctoral fellow at Cornell University with Roald Hoffmann. Keszler's research focuses on inorganic materials for electronic and energy devices.

Head coaches for the 2017 CONCACAF U-20 Championship squads include George Warner (Antigua and Barbuda), Carlos Tábora (Honduras), Marco Antonio Ruiz (Mexico), Wilfrid Montilas (Haiti), Nelson Gallego (Panama), Anthony Isaac (Saint Kitts and Nevis), Tab Ramos (United States), Kyle Lightbourne (Bermuda), Marcelo Herrera (Costa Rica), Eduardo Lara (El Salvador), and Brian Williams (Trinidad and Tobago).

Jabłonna is a village in Legionowo County, Masovian Voivodeship, Poland, with a population of 7,365. It is known for the Jabłonna Palace, a baroque palace built in the late 18th century and now a hotel and event center owned by the Polish Academy of Sciences. The village also contains the Mother of God Queen of Poland Church, originally built in the 1920s and rebuilt after being destroyed in World War II.

The 1999 Brickyard 400 was a NASCAR Winston Cup Series race held at Indianapolis Motor Speedway. The race was won by Dale Jarrett, who led 117 of the 160 laps, becoming the second two-time winner of the event. Jeff Gordon won the pole position. The race was broadcast on ABC with a Nielsen rating of 4.6/15.
Franz Rogowski (born 2 February 1986) is a German film and stage actor, and dancer. Known for *Victoria*, *Transit*, and *Great Freedom*, his work has garnered international acclaim.
Domingos Duarte (born 10 March 1995) is a Portuguese professional footballer who plays as a centre-back for Getafe CF and the Portugal national team.

**NBC Nightside** was an American overnight news program that aired on NBC from November 4, 1991, to September 20, 1998. Produced by NBC News Channel in Charlotte, North Carolina, it was the network's second attempt at a late-night news show, following *NBC News Overnight* (1982–1983). Unlike its competitors, *Nightside* aired seven days a week and featured anchors who later achieved national prominence, such as Antonio Mora and Campbell Brown. Despite its profitability and decent ratings, the program was canceled in 1998.

The Sugar Loaf Islands, known as Ngā Motu in Māori, are a group of islands located near Port Taranaki, New Zealand. The islands are divided into inner and outer groups, with the inner islands including Mataora (Round Rock), Pararaki (Seagull Rock), and Motuotamatea (Snapper Rock), which are connected to the mainland at low tides. The outer islands consist of Motumahanga (Saddleback Island) and Moturoa. Historically significant, Mikotahi was once a half-tide island and the site of the first oil well in the Commonwealth. The islands have served as hunting and gathering grounds for local Māori tribes for centuries. In 1986, the area was designated as a marine park, later reinforced by the Sugar Loaf Islands Marine Park Act in 1991, which protects the islands and surrounding waters from commercial activities. The islands are also notable for their geological significance, representing some of the oldest volcanic activity in the Taranaki region.

*Madden NFL 25* is a 2013 sports video game developed by EA Tiburon and published by EA Sports as part of the *Madden NFL* series. Released for PlayStation 3, Xbox 360, iOS, and Android on August 27, 2013, it later launched as a title for PlayStation 4 and Xbox One. The game introduced the Ignite engine for next-gen consoles, offering enhanced graphics, real-time physics, and improved gameplay mechanics, including more realistic player movements and environmental interactions. It also marked the return of team relocation in the Connected Franchise mode.

Barry Sanders, a Hall of Fame running back, was featured on the cover for seventh-generation consoles, while Adrian Peterson appeared on the next-gen versions. The game received mostly positive reviews for its gameplay improvements but faced criticism for commentary and repetitive elements. Despite mixed reception for its mobile versions, *Madden NFL 25* sold over 3 million copies.
F.C. Netanya is an Israeli football club based in Netanya, founded in 2012, and currently playing in Liga Gimel Sharon.

William Scott Husel, known as Billy Husel, was born on November 11, 1975, in Cleveland, Ohio. A physician, Husel served as an intensive-care doctor at Mount Carmel West Hospital in Columbus, Ohio. From February 2015 to November 2018, he allegedly prescribed excessive fentanyl to patients, resulting in 34 deaths. Accused of 25 counts of murder, Husel was acquitted in April 2022. Following the trial, Husel surrendered his medical license in Ohio, which was officially revoked on May 5, 2022.

Shaarey Zedek Cemetery is a Jewish cemetery in Winnipeg, Manitoba, established in 1894 and owned by Shaarey Zedek Synagogue. The largest Jewish cemetery in the Canadian Prairies, it contains over 8,000 graves, including those of prominent rabbis and the Bronfman family. It features two ohelim (mausoleums), Commonwealth war graves, and a cenotaph honoring Jewish soldiers who died in both World Wars. In 2012, an interfaith burial section, Shaarey Shamayim Cemetery, was added. The cemetery has been the site of historic events, including a "black wedding" during the 1918 flu pandemic, and has faced vandalism in the past.

MV Empire Day was a CAM ship and later a cargo ship built in 1941 by William Doxford & Sons Ltd for the Ministry of War Transport. Commissioned in July 1941, she served in various convoys across the Atlantic and Mediterranean during World War II, transporting supplies and equipment. She was torpedoed and sunk by U-198 on August 7, 1944, in the Indian Ocean, with all 34 crew and eight DEMS gunners surviving.

The Faculty of Social and Human Sciences (Faculdade de Ciências Sociais e Humanas, NOVA FCSH) is a public higher education institution in Lisbon, Portugal, established in 1977 as part of the Universidade NOVA de Lisboa. Located on Avenida de Berna, it offers undergraduate, master's, and doctoral programs across 12 departments, emphasizing interdisciplinary studies in social sciences and humanities. NOVA FCSH is home to 16 research centers, several libraries, and a robust internationalization strategy, including mobility programs like Erasmus.

*Eastwood After Hours: Live at Carnegie Hall* is a live jazz album by Clint Eastwood and The Carnegie Hall Jazz Band, released on April 29, 1997, by Warner Bros. Records. The album was recorded on October 17, 1996, at Carnegie Hall in New York City, and produced by Phil Ramone, Clint Eastwood, and Bruce Ricker.

The album features songs from the soundtracks of Eastwood's films, performed by The Carnegie Hall Jazz Band, a string section, and jazz musicians such as Kenny Barron, Joshua Redman, and Roy Hargrove. The set included "Eastwood: After Hours Suite", featuring piano choruses played by Eastwood. The two-disc set includes archival photographs, film clips, and commentary. *Eastwood After Hours* received positive reviews, with critics praising it as a tribute to Eastwood's cultural legacy.

William Methwold (1590 – 5 March 1653) was an English merchant and agent of the East India Company. He traveled extensively in India, and in 1622, became the first Englishman to visit the Golconda diamond mines. His account of his travels was published in 1626. He served as president of Surat and negotiated the Goa Convention with the Portuguese in 1635. Methwold was elected deputy governor of the East India Company in 1643, and re-elected annually until his death.

The POW/MIA flag is an American flag designed as a symbol of national concern and commitment to resolving the fates of prisoners of war (POW) and those missing in action (MIA). It was created in 1972 by Newt Heisley for the National League of Families of American Prisoners and Missing in Southeast Asia during the Vietnam War. Federal law requires it to be flown at certain federal properties.

The Wesley Bolin Memorial Plaza in Phoenix, Arizona, established in 1978, is an open-air public space honoring Governor Wesley Bolin, located in front of the state capitol. It features 30 memorials, including those for military conflicts and civil rights, and has been a site for protests and gatherings, notably during the 2006 immigration reform rallies. Controversies over some monuments persist.

The International Pool Tour (IPT) shifted its focus to live streaming high-profile matches to promote the sport and increase web traffic. Under Kevin Trudeau's leadership, the IPT introduced a strict dress code for its members and competitors, requiring suits and formal attire to improve the sport's image. Despite deviations from standard pool tournament rules, the IPT was recognized by major associations. However, financial struggles led to the cancellation of a planned championship in London and a failed acquisition deal. Ultimately, the IPT faced insurmountable funding issues, resulting in its demise.
The women's hockey tournament at the 2010 Commonwealth Games featured teams from 10 nations, each led by a head coach, with squad announcements made prior to the event.

Edward A. Keenan (October 15, 1894 – April 12, 1970) was an American politician who served as the 34th mayor of Burlington, Vermont from 1963 to 1965. Prior to being mayor he served on the Burlington Board of Aldermen from 1959 to 1963.

The Council for the Central Laboratory of the Research Councils (CCLRC) was a UK public body (1995–2007) that managed large-scale scientific facilities, including the Diamond Light Source. It merged with PPARC in 2007 to form the Science and Technology Facilities Council (STFC).

The Charleston Dirty Birds are a professional baseball team based in Charleston, West Virginia, and members of the Atlantic League of Professional Baseball. Founded in 1987, the team plays at GoMart Ballpark. The team was formerly known as the West Virginia Power.

An eruption column is a vertical plume of volcanic ash and tephra formed during explosive volcanic activity, where volatile materials in rising magma are disrupted. These columns can reach heights of several kilometers, propelled by strong convection currents, and may be transient or sustained depending on the nature of the eruption. The height of an eruption column is influenced by intrinsic factors such as vent diameter and gas content, as well as extrinsic factors like wind and atmospheric temperature gradients. Eruption columns can inject particulates into the stratosphere, potentially causing global temperature changes, as seen after the 1991 Mount Pinatubo eruption. Additionally, column collapse can lead to dangerous pyroclastic flows. Eruption columns pose significant hazards to aviation, prompting the establishment of monitoring networks to mitigate risks associated with volcanic ash.
Aberdare Girls' School was a secondary school in Aberdare, Wales, established in 1913 and closed in 2014. It merged with two other schools to form Aberdare Community School.

Erik Rauch (May 15, 1974 – July 13, 2005) was an American biophysicist and theoretical ecologist known for founding MetaCarta and contributing to spatial information processing systems. He earned degrees from Yale University, Stanford University, and the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, and held positions at several prestigious institutions before his untimely death in a hiking accident.
Deitingen is a municipality in the district of Wasseramt, in the canton of Solothurn, Switzerland.

Kuressaare is a town located on Saaremaa Island in Estonia, with a population of 12,698 as of 2021. First documented in 1154, it developed around the medieval Kuressaare Castle, which now houses the Saaremaa Regional Museum. Historically known as Arensburg, the town has undergone various name changes and governance, including periods under Danish, Swedish, and Russian control. Kuressaare is recognized for its cultural events, such as the Saaremaa Opera Days, and serves as a popular seaside resort.

The Limestone Coast is a region in southeastern South Australia, known for its unique geology, wine production, and coastal landscapes. It includes towns such as Mount Gambier, Naracoorte, and Robe, as well as significant wetlands and the World Heritage-listed Naracoorte Caves. The area features Mediterranean climate, viticulture, and diverse wildlife, though much of its natural habitat has been altered by agriculture.

Sir Richard Vickers (born 21 August 1928) was a British Army officer who served from 1948 to 1983, reaching the rank of Lieutenant-General and commanding various units including The Royal Dragoons and the Blues and Royals. He was also a Knight Commander of the Order of the Bath and a Commander of the Royal Victorian Order.
The Willard Gallery was an art gallery in New York City founded in 1940 by Marian Guthrie Willard. It closed in 1987.
Michael Atwell Slater (died February 2, 1842) was a British Royal Navy officer and hydrographer. Promoted to Commander in 1837, he contributed to Admiralty Charts and coastal surveys of Britain.

Victory Day over Nazism in World War II (Ukrainian: День перемоги над нацизмом у Другій світовій війні) is a Ukrainian holiday celebrated annually on May 9. Established in 2015, it replaced the Soviet-era Victory Day, following the country's decommunization laws. The holiday aims to shift the focus from glorifying war to honoring personal histories, similar to Remembrance Day. The remembrance poppy is the official symbol.

Augustus Jones (c. 1757–1836) was a prominent land surveyor and farmer who played a crucial role in shaping the geography and politics of southern Ontario. Born in the Hudson River Valley, Jones trained as a surveyor in New York City before fleeing to Upper Canada with his family in the 1780s due to his loyalty to the British Crown. He served as the Deputy Surveyor General for the Nassau District (Home District) from 1789 to 1799, surveying numerous townships and roads, including the famous Jones Baseline and the initial route of Yonge Street. Jones also held positions as a magistrate and militia captain, and was known for his fluency in indigenous languages and his strong relationships with local Native communities. After retiring from surveying in 1800, he became a farmer and moved to Brantford in 1817. He is remembered for his extensive contributions to the development of Ontario, and a statue honoring his work was unveiled in Stoney Creek in 2005.

The 1952–53 FC Basel season was marked by significant success, as the team, managed by René Bader and Willy Dürr, won the Nationalliga A championship, finishing four points ahead of second-placed Young Boys. Basel played a total of 43 matches, achieving 27 wins, and reached the quarter-finals of the Swiss Cup. Josef Hügi was the top scorer, netting 32 league goals.
Kleiner Tiergarten, an urban park in Moabit, Berlin, covers 7 hectares and has a history dating back to 1655, with notable redesigns in 1835 and 1960.

Leif Carlsen (4 December 1943 – 14 September 2018) was a Danish footballer who played as a forward for Boldklubben Frem, Hvidovre IF, and Landskrona BoIS, helping the latter secure promotion to Sweden's top division.

Sergio Llull (born November 15, 1987) is a Spanish professional basketball player for Real Madrid of the Liga ACB and the EuroLeague. A versatile guard, he has played for Real Madrid since 2007, winning numerous titles, including two EuroLeague championships (2015, 2018) and seven Liga ACB titles. Llull was named EuroLeague MVP in 2017 and has earned multiple individual accolades throughout his career.

Selected in the 2009 NBA draft, his rights are currently held by the New York Knicks. Llull has also been a key player for the Spanish national team, winning gold at the 2019 FIBA World Cup and three EuroBasket championships.

Local councils in Israel are municipal units designated for settlements with populations exceeding 2,000, but not large enough to be classified as cities. The Israeli Interior Minister determines the status of these councils, considering residents' preferences regarding their classification. Local councils play a significant role in town planning and are represented by the Union of Local Authorities in Israel (ULAI), established in 1938 to advocate for local councils at the national level.

Ruth Ella Moore (May 19, 1903 – July 19, 1994) was an American bacteriologist and microbiologist who became the first African-American woman to earn a Ph.D. in the natural sciences. She received her doctorate in bacteriology from Ohio State University in 1933, where her research focused on *Mycobacterium tuberculosis*. Moore later joined Howard University as a professor and became the first woman to chair its bacteriology department, which she renamed the Department of Microbiology.  

Her scientific contributions included studies on blood types, dental caries, and antibiotic resistance in gut microbiomes. She was also the first African-American member of the American Society for Microbiology. Beyond her scientific career, Moore was an accomplished fashion designer, creating her own garments. She received multiple honors, including honorary degrees and recognition from Ohio State University. Moore died in 1994 at the age of 91.

"Songs of the Pixies" is a poem by Samuel Taylor Coleridge, written in 1793 during a summer vacation in Devon. Inspired by local folklore and the Pixies' Parlour near Ottery, the poem blends themes of fairies, mythology, and poetic self-reflection. It was first published in Coleridge's 1796 *Poems on Various Subjects* but later dismissed by the poet himself. The work draws on influences from Milton, Shakespeare, and other literary figures, showcasing Coleridge's early imaginative style. While receiving mixed critical responses, it is noted for its inventive imagery and its reflection of Coleridge's evolving poetic identity.

Might and Magic: Heroes Kingdoms is a real-time strategy massively multiplayer online game developed and published by Ubisoft. Released in 2009, it features a browser-based gameplay where players manage cities, train troops, and compete in alliances to collect resources and conquer regions. The game operated until August 2014 when its servers were permanently closed.
Apollonius Dyscolus was a 2nd-century Alexandrian grammarian known for founding scientific syntax and influencing later grammatical traditions.

Marahoué National Park is a national park in central Ivory Coast, established in 1968. It originally contained a diversity of habitats, including dense forest and savanna. Illegal logging and settling have resulted in almost complete deforestation and conversion to agricultural land. The park was once home to chimpanzees, but they are now extirpated.

Killing Birds is a 1988 Italian horror film directed by Claudio Lattanzi and produced by Aristide Massaccesi. The film follows a group of college students assigned to find a rare bird species, only to encounter a series of terrifying events and zombie-like creatures. Set against the backdrop of a haunted house, the survivors face deadly threats as they struggle to survive. The movie blends elements of horror and supernatural occurrences, culminating in a tense and chilling finale.

"Burma VJ" is a Danish documentary film directed by Anders Østergaard, released on November 12, 2008. The film chronicles the 2007 Burmese protests through the eyes of local video journalists, highlighting issues of censorship and the struggle for freedom of speech. It received critical acclaim, boasting a 97% approval rating on Rotten Tomatoes and winning several awards, including the Golden Apricot for Best Documentary Film at the 2009 Yerevan International Film Festival.

The Arnot Art Museum is a historic art museum in Elmira, New York, housed in a Greek Revival mansion built in 1833. Founded by banker Matthias H. Arnot, it features 19th-century European and American art, including works by Bouguereau, Bierstadt, Cole, and Millet. The museum expanded in 1982 and remains a contributing property to the Elmira Civic Historic District.
Ciudad del Plata is a city in San José Department, Uruguay, established in 2006.

Connah's Quay Power Station in Flintshire, Wales, is a 1,420 MW natural gas-fired power station. Originally a coal-fired station opened in 1954, it was replaced by a CCGT station in 1996, now owned by Uniper.

The Apostles' Fast is a Christian fasting period observed by various Orthodox traditions, beginning on the Monday after All Saints' Sunday and lasting until June 29, the Feast of the Holy Apostles Peter and Paul. Instituted as a preparation for the apostles' missionary work following the Resurrection of Jesus, the Fast is rooted in scriptural references where Jesus speaks of the need for fasting after his departure. While not as strict as other fasting periods, it typically involves abstaining from red meat, poultry, dairy, and eggs, with some variations among different Orthodox communities. The duration of the Fast can range from eight to 42 days, depending on the date of Pascha (Easter).
Tim Hauck (born December 20, 1966) is a former American football safety who played 13 seasons in the NFL and later became a coach. He won Super Bowl LII as safeties coach for the Philadelphia Eagles.

A three-point turn is a driving maneuver used to turn a vehicle around in a narrow space, typically involving three distinct movements: driving across the road, reversing, and then driving forward again. While commonly referred to as a "three-point turn" in several countries, it is known by different names in various regions, such as "turning in the road" in the UK.

Frederick Twort (22 October 1877 – 20 March 1950) was an English bacteriologist known for his discovery of bacteriophages, viruses that infect and destroy bacteria. Educated at St Thomas's Hospital, he became superintendent of the Brown Animal Sanatory Institution, where he conducted groundbreaking research on bacterial mutation, growth factors, and diseases like leprosy and Johne's disease. His 1915 discovery of bacteriophages was initially overlooked but later recognized as a major contribution to microbiology. Twort was elected a Fellow of the Royal Society in 1929.

Saint Melchior is one of the traditional names attributed to one of the Three Magi, or Wise Men, who visited Jesus after his birth, as described in the New Testament. Often depicted as an elderly king from Persia, Melchior is recognized for presenting gold to signify Jesus' kingship. He is venerated in various Christian denominations and commemorated on January 1 and January 6, with his remains housed in the Shrine of the Three Kings at Cologne Cathedral.

Prakash Nanjappa (born 29 February 1976) is an Indian shooter known for competing in the 10 metre air pistol and 50 metre pistol events. He began shooting in 1999 and gained prominence after winning a bronze medal at the 2013 ISSF World Cup in Changwon. Nanjappa also secured a silver medal at the 2014 Commonwealth Games in Glasgow. He qualified for the 2016 Rio Olympics, finishing 25th in the qualification round. Nanjappa is the son of national-level shooter P. N. Papanna.

Courtney Andrew Walsh (born October 30, 1962) is a former West Indian cricketer and one of the most celebrated fast bowlers in the history of the sport. He played international cricket for the West Indies from 1984 to 2001, participating in 132 Test matches and 205 One Day Internationals (ODIs). Walsh is renowned for his exceptional bowling skills, having taken 519 Test wickets, making him the first bowler to reach this milestone, and 227 ODI wickets. His career highlights include being named one of the Wisden Cricketers of the Year in 1987 and achieving a remarkable five-wicket haul of 5 runs against Sri Lanka in 1986. After retiring, Walsh transitioned into coaching, serving as a selector for the West Indies national team and later as a bowling coach for Bangladesh and the West Indies women's team. He was inducted into the ICC Cricket Hall of Fame in 2010, solidifying his legacy as one of cricket's greats.

Robert Henry Doolan (1917–2022) was an American World War II veteran and former prisoner of war. A navigator in the U.S. Army Air Corps, he flew 12 combat missions before being shot down on his 13th mission in 1943. After evading capture for 21 days with the help of the Dutch resistance, he was apprehended by the Gestapo and spent two years in the Stalag Luft III prison camp, famously depicted in *The Great Escape*. Liberated in 1945, Doolan returned to the United States, where he pursued a career in civil engineering. He lived to the age of 105.

Sir Constantijn Huygens (1596–1687) was a Dutch Golden Age poet, composer, architect, diplomat, and scholar. He was secretary to two Princes of Orange, and father to the scientist Christiaan Huygens. Huygens was knighted by James I and published *Pathodia sacra et profana* in 1647.

The University of Florida clinical toxicology distance education program is an online graduate program offered by the UF College of Pharmacy. It includes a Master of Science (MS) in Clinical Toxicology, a 32-credit degree for health professionals, and a 15-credit graduate certificate in clinical toxicology. Both programs focus on medical and toxicological principles related to toxicants, drug analysis, and overdose treatment. The MS program is designed for poison control specialists, pharmacists, nurses, physicians, and emergency responders, while the certificate provides foundational knowledge for similar professionals. The program is entirely online, allowing working professionals to advance their expertise in clinical toxicology.

Helene Hewitt, born Helene Theresa Banks, is a meteorologist and oceanographer. She studied mathematics at Cambridge University and the University of Southampton, and later worked at the Woods Hole Oceanographic Institution and the Met Office. Hewitt has developed and assessed climate models, including HadGEM3, and coordinated the IPCC Sixth Assessment Report on Ocean, Cryosphere, and Sea Level Change. She was appointed Officer of the Order of the British Empire in 2022.

Sapporo Television Broadcasting Co., Ltd. (STV) is a private television and radio broadcasting company based in Sapporo, Japan. Founded on April 8, 1958, it began television broadcasts on April 1, 1959, initially as a dual affiliate of NNN and FNN. STV later became a founding member of NNN in 1966 and transitioned to color broadcasts the same year. It also played a key role in broadcasting the 1972 Sapporo Winter Olympics. 

STV launched its radio operations in 1962, becoming the only Japanese broadcaster to start with television before radio. The company introduced digital broadcasting in 2006 and ended analog broadcasts in 2011. Known for its innovative approaches, such as adopting an automatic advertising broadcast system, STV has consistently ranked first in TV ratings since 1993, holding the longest record for all-day ratings in Japan.

Tomás Francisco Reilly (December 20, 1908 – July 21, 1992) was an American-born Catholic bishop. He was the first Bishop of San Juan de la Maguana in the Dominican Republic, serving from 1969 to 1977. Earlier, he was Titular Bishop of Themisonium and Prelate of San Juan de la Maguana from 1956.

The Black Country derby is a football rivalry between West Bromwich Albion and Wolverhampton Wanderers, two clubs located in the Black Country region of England. One of the oldest derbies in league football, it began in 1883 and has seen a total of 162 meetings, with Albion winning 65 times and Wolves 53. The rivalry peaked in the 1950s when both teams competed at the top level, and it has continued to evoke strong local passion, despite fluctuations in league status.

Canajoharie Creek is a river in Central New York, United States. It is located in Otsego and Montgomery counties. The creek originates north of Cherry Valley and flows northward, eventually converging with the Mohawk River in Canajoharie.

The creek features two waterfalls, Judds Falls and Canajoharie Falls, and a deep pothole known as "the boiling pot." The name "Canajoharie" is derived from a Mohawk term referring to this pothole. The creek has a history of flash flooding, and the United States Geological Survey maintains stream gauges along its course. The creek is stocked with brown trout and is rated Class C for water quality.

The 19th Reserve Division was a German infantry division that fought in World War I on the Western and Eastern Fronts. It was active from 1914 to 1919 and had approximately 15,000 soldiers. The division fought in many major battles, including the First Battle of the Marne, the Battle of Verdun, the Battle of the Somme, Passchendaele, and the Oise-Aisne Offensive. Allied intelligence considered the division to be first class.

Bader Philanthropies, founded in 1992, is a private charitable foundation headquartered in Milwaukee, Wisconsin. It combines the Helen Daniels Bader Fund, focusing on Alzheimer's and elder health, with the Isabel and Alfred Bader Fund, which supports low-income Milwaukeeans and Jewish education. Since its inception, the organization has awarded over $400 million in grants and program-related investments across various areas.

Murayama Tōan (1555–1619) was a wealthy merchant and influential figure in Nagasaki who became a key figure in Japanese-Spanish and Japanese-Chinese trade. Appointed Magistrate of Nagasaki, he supported direct trading relations with China and led a failed 1616 expedition to Taiwan. He faced accusations of harboring priests and was executed in 1619 along with most of his family.

Getafe Air Base, located in Getafe, Spain, was built in 1911 and serves as a military airbase operated by the Spanish Air and Space Force. It hosts the "Ala 35" transport wing, which operates CASA/IPTN CN-235 and EADS CASA C-295 aircraft. The base also houses maintenance facilities for various aircraft and programs for their improvement and modernization. Adjacent to the airbase, EADS CASA assembles Spanish Eurofighter Typhoons. Plans for converting part of the base into a civil aerodrome for general aviation and low-cost airlines have faced opposition from the City of Getafe due to environmental concerns. The base remains integral to the city's industrial-aeronautical fabric.

MV *Empire Day* was a British CAM ship and later cargo vessel built in 1941 for the Ministry of War Transport. Operated by Stephens Sutton Ltd and later Lyle Shipping Co Ltd, she carried a Hawker Sea Hurricane and defensive armament for convoy protection. During World War II, she transported supplies across the Atlantic, Mediterranean, and Indian Ocean. On 7 August 1944, she was torpedoed and sunk by U-198 off East Africa, though all but one of her crew survived.

Anne Line Gjersem, born on January 6, 1994, in Hønefoss, Norway, is a former Norwegian figure skater. She competed internationally from 2008 to 2018, representing Norway in the 2014 Winter Olympics, where she placed 23rd. Gjersem won multiple national championships and achieved her best world championship result of 22nd in 2015. She retired from competitive skating on June 4, 2018.

The Lincoln Home National Historic Site in Springfield, Illinois, preserves the only home ever owned by Abraham Lincoln. Purchased in 1844, it was where he lived before becoming president. The site, established in 1971, includes the restored house and surrounding neighborhood, administered by the National Park Service.

"L.A. Law: The Movie," also known as "L.A. Law: Return to Justice," is a 2002 drama film that serves as a reunion for the television series "L.A. Law." Directed by Michael Schultz and written by William M. Finkelstein, the film follows the dynamics at the law firm McKenzie Brackman, where new challenges arise as former partners and employees navigate personal and professional conflicts, including a high-stakes legal case involving a former client facing execution.

The Grand Loop Road is a historic roadway in Yellowstone National Park, Wyoming, that forms a figure-eight loop connecting many of the park's most notable natural features and landmarks. Constructed by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers and later maintained by the National Park Service, the road was added to the National Register of Historic Places on December 23, 2003. 

The route begins near Mammoth Hot Springs at the park's headquarters and traverses diverse landscapes, including Golden Gate Canyon, Obsidian Canyon, and the Norris Geyser Basin. It passes iconic sites such as Sheepeater Cliff, Old Faithful in the Upper Geyser Basin, Yellowstone Lake, and the Grand Canyon of the Yellowstone. The road also crosses the Continental Divide and Dunraven Pass, offering access to geysers, waterfalls, and scenic valleys. 

The Grand Loop Road connects with several entrance roads, providing access to neighboring areas like Grand Teton National Park and Cooke City, Montana, making it a central feature of Yellowstone's infrastructure.

The Catskill Mountain House was a hotel built in 1824 on a plateau in the Catskill Mountains. It became a popular subject for artists and writers, including those of the Hudson River School. The hotel declined in popularity and was burned down by the State of New York in 1963. Today, the site offers sweeping views and is near the North-South Lake campground.

Clyde Lee Chambliss Jr. (born April 16, 1969) is an American politician serving as a Republican member of the Alabama Senate from the 30th district since 2014. In 2019, he sponsored the Human Life Protection Act, a controversial abortion law banning most abortions in Alabama, with exceptions only for cases where the mother’s health is endangered.

TVR Cultural is a Romanian television channel dedicated to culture and the arts, originally launched in 2002 by Televiziunea Română. It ceased broadcasting in 2012 due to financial difficulties but was relaunched in 2022, offering programming in both SD and HD formats.

In literary theory, polyphony is a narrative structure where multiple voices and perspectives are presented without a single, dominant authorial voice imposing a unified view. This concept, developed by Mikhail Bakhtin, contrasts with monologic narratives, where the author's ideology controls the work and characters' ideas are either affirmed or repudiated.

In a polyphonic work, characters possess their own "voice-ideas," an artistic fusion of personality and idea, allowing them to "signify directly." These voices are "unmerged" and cannot be contained within a single consciousness, even when they agree. The author relinquishes control, confronting characters as equals and participating in a "great dialogue" where the outcome is not predetermined. This creates an "open-ended dialogue" that reflects the true, lived nature of human existence, where unforeseen possibilities arise from the interaction of autonomous consciousnesses. Bakhtin argues that polyphony is not merely an artistic technique but a reflection of a broader cultural shift away from monologic thinking.

Eastern Macedonia and Thrace is an administrative region in northeastern Greece, established in 1987 and restructured under the 2010 Kallikratis plan. Its capital is Komotini, with Alexandroupolis as the largest city. The region comprises six regional units and is home to Greece's main Muslim minority.
Tome VI is a 1968 jazz album by Gil Mellé, released on Verve Records. It features electronic effects and a revamped quartet, marking a technical breakthrough in live electronic jazz performances.

*The Gateway* (originally titled *Curtains*) is a 2015 horror film that follows Danni, a former hospice nurse turned whale conservation activist, who discovers a mysterious portal in her shower. Her investigation leads to deadly consequences, including encounters with monsters and a sinister figure known as the Pale Man. The film received mixed reviews, with critics praising its originality and charm but noting its limited appeal and unconventional narrative.
Kaberamaido is a town in Eastern Uganda, located in Kaberamaido District. With an estimated population of 3,400 (2011), it lies about 163 km northwest of Mbale and 64 km west of Soroti.

Andrea Quynhgiao Nguyen (born 1969) is an American food writer and cookbook author known for her expertise in Vietnamese cuisine. Born in Hải Dương, Vietnam, she emigrated to the United States with her family after the fall of Saigon in 1975. Nguyen has authored several acclaimed cookbooks, including *Into the Vietnamese Kitchen* and *The Pho Cookbook*, the latter winning a James Beard Award in 2018. She has contributed to various publications and teaches cooking classes across the U.S.

USS *Arizona* (1858) was a sidewheel steamer built in Wilmington, Delaware, for commercial use before being seized by the Confederacy during the American Civil War and renamed *Caroline* for blockade-running. Captured by the Union Navy in 1862, she was recommissioned as USS *Arizona* in 1863 and served in the West Gulf Blockading Squadron, participating in key operations such as the Battle of Grand Lake and the Red River Campaign. She also supported Union efforts at Port Hudson and Sabine Pass. The vessel was destroyed by fire in 1865 near New Orleans. Efforts to locate and preserve her wreck are ongoing through the USS Arizona Civil War Project Fund.

The VENUE sound system is a series of digital mixing consoles developed by Avid Technology, designed for live sound applications. The range includes several models, starting with the D-Show, the first and largest control surface, which was discontinued in 2015. The Profile model became a standard in the industry for touring and festivals, while the SC48 offered an all-in-one solution with built-in processing and I/O capabilities. The S3L-X, introduced in 2013, featured a compact design and modular architecture, allowing for extensive connectivity and integration with digital audio workstations. The flagship S6L, released in 2015, replaced earlier models and introduced advanced features such as multiple touch screens, enhanced processing power, and support for high-performance audio paths. All VENUE consoles are compatible with Pro Tools for direct recording and offer inter-compatibility of show files, although some adjustments may be necessary due to architectural differences.

St. Mary's High School, located in Belagavi, Karnataka, India, was established in 1851. It serves as a coeducational institution with a long history and a current enrollment of 2,496 students. The school offers a diverse range of co-curricular activities, including sports, literary clubs, and various examinations. Notable events include the annual Passing Out parade and participation in the Royceton Memorial Trophy. The school's campus spans 2.08 acres and is managed by Philips Alvaris, the current headmaster.

Mutational meltdown is a process in population genetics where the accumulation of deleterious mutations in a small population leads to a decline in population size and growth rate, ultimately resulting in extinction. This occurs as harmful mutations become fixed in the population, reducing fitness and increasing mortality rates. The process is particularly rapid in small populations due to genetic drift and the shorter time required for mutations to become fixed.

The effects of mutational meltdown differ between asexual and sexual populations. Asexual populations are more vulnerable due to the lack of genetic recombination, which allows mutations to accumulate more quickly. In contrast, sexual populations benefit from genetic diversity and recombination, which can slow or prevent mutational meltdown. However, under certain conditions, such as low birth rates or population bottlenecks, even large sexually reproducing populations can experience mutational meltdown.

Sanjog is a 1972 Indian Hindi film directed by S. S. Balan, featuring Amitabh Bachchan and Mala Sinha. The plot follows Mohan and Asha, whose marriage faces familial opposition, leading to separation. Years later, they reunite under complicated circumstances, culminating in tragedy.

Matthew Seers (born 28 June 1974) is a former Australian rugby league player who played as a fullback, centre, and five-eighth. He had a notable career with Norths Devils from 1993 to 1999, representing Country Origin and New South Wales. Seers also played for Wests Tigers, Wakefield Trinity Wildcats, and Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks. He faced legal troubles in 2015 for drug offenses.

Merewether is a coastal suburb of Newcastle, New South Wales, Australia, established in 1890 and named after Edward Christopher Merewether. Known for its coal mining history, the area also features iconic beaches, including Merewether Beach and the Merewether Ocean Baths, the largest in the Southern Hemisphere. It is a hub for surfing, hosting Newcastle Surfest, and is home to professional surfer Mark Richards. The suburb includes several schools, historic sites, and recreational facilities, blending its industrial past with modern residential and leisure developments.

Pivotal (19 January 1993 – 19 November 2021) was a British Thoroughbred racehorse and influential sire. Bred and owned by Cheveley Park Stud, he was trained by Sir Mark Prescott and had a racing record of six starts, including major wins in the 1996 King's Stand Stakes and Nunthorpe Stakes. Retired to stud in 1997, Pivotal became a highly successful breeding stallion, siring numerous major winners. He was retired from stud duties in 2021 and died later that year.

A fish flake is a traditional structure used for drying cod, consisting of a horizontal framework of small poles supported by upright poles. Located near fishing stages, flakes were either permanent or temporary (hand flakes) and essential for preserving fish through natural drying. First noted in the 1770s, these structures were widely used until the mid-20th century, when cold storage and freezers reduced their necessity. The drying process involved spreading salted cod on the flakes, alternating sides to expose them to sunlight, and storing them in fish stores until market readiness.

"Let It Go" is a single by Hong Kong singer Jacky Cheung, released in April 2010 as part of his album *Private Corner*. The song, which blends jazz and canto-jazz, features lyrics adapted into Cantonese by Kenny So, while the chorus retains the English phrase "Let It Go." Produced by Andrew Tuason, the track includes a gospel choir and achieved notable success, reaching number 16 on the HMVHK sales chart.

The 1923 Chicago mayoral election saw Democrat William E. Dever defeat Republican Arthur C. Lueder with 52.21% of the vote, marking the first Catholic mayor of Chicago. Dever, a reformer supported by progressive independents, won strong backing from ethnic communities and African Americans, defeating Lueder, who focused on administrative experience.

The C. A. Holliday Transfer Facility is a Texas Department of Criminal Justice (TDCJ) transfer unit for male inmates located in Huntsville, Texas. Named for C. A. Holliday, an African-American community activist and pastor, the facility opened in January 1994. The prison was the result of an effort led by Richard Watkins, a senior African-American prison warden, who advocated for a prison to be named in Holliday's honor. The facility has a capacity of around 2,000 beds.

"Cookie" is a song by South Korean girl group NewJeans, released on August 1, 2022, as the third single from their debut self-titled extended play. While praised for its production and groove, the song faced controversy over alleged sexual innuendos in its lyrics, which some deemed inappropriate for a group of minors. NewJeans' agency, ADOR, denied the allegations, asserting the lyrics were intended for fans and lacked sexual connotations. The group promoted the song during their debut stage on *M Countdown*.

The Oetker Collection is a luxury hospitality company founded in 1870 and headquartered in Baden-Baden, Germany. Managed by the Oetker family, it operates a portfolio of prestigious hotels and country estates, including Brenners Park-Hotel & Spa, Hotel Le Bristol in Paris, and Hôtel du Cap-Eden-Roc. The company has pursued ambitious expansion since 2008 under CEO Timo Gruenert.

Acoustic torpedo  

An acoustic torpedo is a self-guided underwater missile that uses sonar to detect and home in on a target, typically a ship or submarine. These torpedoes employ passive sonar to detect noise from the target (such as engine or propeller sounds) or active sonar to reflect pulses off the target. Early models had fixed sensor patterns, while modern versions use computer-controlled systems for greater precision.  

First deployed in World War II, the German G7es (T5) torpedo proved highly effective against Allied ships, forcing the development of countermeasures like the Foxer noisemaker, which created distracting cavitation noise to lure torpedoes away. Allied tactics, such as the "Step-Aside" maneuver, also reduced their effectiveness. Postwar advancements led to improved decoy systems like the AN/SLQ-25 Nixie and AN/SLQ-61 Lightweight Tow.  

Captured German technology, including from U-505 and U-250, provided valuable insights into acoustic torpedo design. Despite initial setbacks, countermeasures significantly reduced their wartime success rate, with only about 77 out of 700 German torpedoes hitting their targets. Today, acoustic torpedoes remain a key component of naval warfare, with ongoing improvements in guidance and evasion tactics.

The 2010 conservatism-psychoticism correlation error refers to a mistake in a study by Brad Verhulst, Peter K. Hatemi, and Nicholas G. Martin, which incorrectly reported positive correlations between psychoticism and conservative attitudes. The error, later repeated in subsequent publications, reversed the actual negative correlations. While the mistake did not affect the study's primary thesis, it caused confusion in later research and public discourse. The error was eventually corrected after scrutiny by researchers, sparking debates over data transparency and the interpretation of psychoticism in political psychology.

Brodie Smith (born in Jacksonville, Florida) is an American professional Ultimate Disc League player, disc golfer, and trick shot performer. He began playing ultimate frisbee at Nease High School in Ponte Vedra, Florida, and later joined the University of Florida's ultimate team in 2006. During his college career, the team won two National College Championships, in 2006 and 2010. 

Smith started his professional ultimate career in 2011 with the Indianapolis Alleycats of the American Ultimate Disc League (AUDL) and later played for teams including the Chicago Wildfire, Dallas Roughnecks, and Jacksonville Cannons. His career was interrupted by injuries, including a torn ACL and meniscus, which sidelined him for two seasons.

Smith gained widespread recognition for his frisbee trick shot videos, starting with his 2011 YouTube video "Frisbee Trick Shots," which garnered millions of views. His creative and challenging trick shots have been featured on ESPN SportsCenter's Top Plays and have helped promote awareness of ultimate frisbee as a sport. Smith has performed trick shots worldwide, collaborated with YouTube group Dude Perfect, and appeared on a Japanese game show. He continues to share his content on social media platforms, inspiring fans globally.

"Flash" is a song by the Japanese girl group Perfume, released as a single on March 16, 2016, and later included in their album *Cosmic Explorer*. Produced by Yasutaka Nakata, the song blends J-pop and EDM elements and features choreography inspired by martial arts and Tai chi. Its music video, directed by Yusuke Tanaka, incorporates CGI and LED light sabers, becoming Perfume's most-watched video on YouTube with over 60 million views.

Clepsicosma iridia, described by Edward Meyrick in 1888, is a species of moth endemic to New Zealand. Found in both the North and South Islands, particularly in native forests, it is common and has a flight period from late December to May. The species' forewing features a wavy line of iridescent scales, named after the Roman goddess Iris. Clepsicosma iridia is believed to feed on species of Cutty grass, though this has not been confirmed through captive rearing. The moths are nocturnal and attracted to light, often resting under leaves during the day.

Ahmad bin Ali Al Thani (1922–1977) was the Emir of Qatar from 1960 to 1972. He ascended to the throne after his father, Sheikh Ali bin Abdullah Al Thani, abdicated. Ahmad oversaw Qatar's independence from Britain in 1971 and implemented some economic reforms, including establishing key government departments. However, he was deposed in a bloodless coup in 1972 by his cousin, Khalifa bin Hamad Al Thani. Ahmad lived in exile in Dubai until his death in London in 1977, after which he was buried in Al Rayyan Cemetery in Qatar.

The Aeroprakt A-22 Foxbat is a light aircraft designed and manufactured by Aeroprakt in Kyiv, Ukraine. Development began in February 1990, with the first prototype flying on October 21, 1996, and production of a German-certified version starting in 1999. The A-22 features a predominantly metal structure, excellent visibility through extensive glazing, and a 3-axis control system. It is equipped with flaperons, allowing for a low stall speed of 52 km/h (32 mph). The aircraft can be powered by various engines, including the Rotax 912UL and Jabiru 2200.

The Captain Benjamin Williams House is a historic Georgian-style home in Middletown, Connecticut, built around 1797. Located at 27 Washington Street, it features brick construction, brownstone detailing, and a hip roof with dormers. Originally owned by sea captain Benjamin Williams, it later belonged to the deKoven family before being restored in the 20th century. The house was added to the National Register of Historic Places in 1978 and is part of the Middletown Heritage Trail.

Oscar Braynon (born February 1, 1977) is an American Democratic politician who served in the Florida Senate from 2011 to 2020, representing parts of Miami-Dade County. He was Senate Minority Leader from 2016 to 2018. Previously, he served in the Florida House of Representatives (2008–2011) and on the Miami Gardens City Council (2003–2008).

John Thomas Gordon Ruddy (born 24 October 1986) is an English professional footballer who plays as a goalkeeper for Championship club Birmingham City.

Ruddy began his career at Cambridge United, making his professional debut in 2004. In 2005, he moved to Everton, where he made only one senior appearance, spending much of his time on loan at various clubs, including Walsall, Rushden & Diamonds, Chester City, Stockport County, Wrexham, Bristol City and Crewe Alexandra. In 2009, he joined Motherwell on loan, where he made 34 appearances.

In 2010, Ruddy signed for Norwich City, where he became a first-team regular and helped the club gain promotion to the Premier League. He was voted the club's Player's Player of the Season in 2012 and earned a call-up to the England squad for UEFA Euro 2012. After Norwich were relegated from the Premier League, Ruddy stayed with the club and helped them bounce back via the play-offs.

In 2017, Ruddy joined Wolverhampton Wanderers, where he won the Championship title and was named in the PFA Team of the Year. After five years with Wolves, he signed for Birmingham City in 2022.

Ruddy made his senior debut for England in 2012 in a friendly against Italy.

"The Whole Family" is a collaborative novel published in 1908, conceived by William Dean Howells as a showcase of literary realism. The book features contributions from various authors, including Henry James and Mary E. Wilkins Freeman, who each explore the dynamics of the Talbert family, middle-class proprietors of a silverplate works. The narrative begins with Peggy Talbert's engagement to Harry Goward, but as the story unfolds, family tensions and rivalries emerge, particularly surrounding the character of Aunt Elizabeth, who is reimagined by Freeman as a vibrant and alluring woman. The novel was serialized in Harper's Bazar and received a favorable contemporary reception, noted for its unique collaborative nature and the interplay of differing authorial visions. Despite its initial success, the project faced challenges, including disputes over character portrayals and author compensation, reflecting the complexities of both family and artistic collaboration.

Mafdet was an ancient Egyptian goddess associated with protection, justice, and execution. She was believed to defend the sun god Ra during his nightly voyage, earning the epithet "Piercer of Darkness," and played a role in reassembling Osiris after his dismemberment. Mafdet was often depicted as a feline or mongoose, a woman with the head of such an animal, or an animal with a woman's head, with the feline commonly interpreted as a cheetah or serval. In art, she was shown running up the side of an executioner's staff, symbolizing her role in delivering justice by ripping out the hearts of wrongdoers and presenting them to the pharaoh. During the New Kingdom, she was associated with the judgment hall in Duat, where she executed the enemies of the pharaoh.

Mordaunt Fenwick-Bisset (died July 7, 1884) was an Irish landowner and politician who served as a Member of Parliament for West Somerset from 1880 until his resignation in 1883. He was also a prominent figure in staghunting, serving as Master of the Devon and Somerset Staghounds from 1855 to 1881, where he played a key role in revitalizing the sport and increasing wild red deer populations on Exmoor.

Massachusetts Route 225, maintained by MassDOT, stretches 31.100 miles from Route 2A in Lunenburg to Route 2 in Lexington. It passes through Worcester and Middlesex counties, concurrent with Routes 111 and 119 in parts of Groton and Westford. Route 225 was renumbered from Route 25 in 1957 and now ends where I-495 and Route 110 intersect in Groton.

Summoning is an atmospheric black metal band formed in 1993 in Vienna, Austria, by members Silenius (Michael Gregor), Protector (Richard Lederer), and Trifixion (Alexander Trondl). Known for their epic and hypnotic sound, the band initially released traditional black metal music with their debut album, *Lugburz*, in 1995. Over the years, Summoning evolved their style, incorporating keyboards and choir vocals, heavily influenced by the works of J.R.R. Tolkien. The band has been active since its formation, with notable albums including *Minas Morgul*, *Oath Bound*, and *With Doom We Come*, released in 2018.

USS *Housatonic* (SP-1697) was a freighter launched in 1899 as *El Rio* by Newport News Shipbuilding for the Morgan Line. Originally serving as a commercial vessel between New York and Gulf Coast ports, it was acquired by the U.S. Navy in 1917 during World War I and converted into a minelayer. Commissioned on January 25, 1918, *Housatonic* laid 9,339 mines as part of the North Sea Mine Barrage and later transported American troops home after the war. Decommissioned in 1919, it returned to commercial service, renamed *Brazos* in 1925. The ship was lost in a collision with HMS *Archer* on January 13, 1942, during World War II.

Tomita–Takesaki theory is a fundamental framework in the study of von Neumann algebras, focusing on the modular automorphisms of states. It introduces key concepts such as the modular operator and modular conjugation, which arise from the polar decomposition of certain antilinear operators associated with a cyclic and separating vector in a Hilbert space. The theory establishes a 1-parameter group of modular automorphisms linked to a state, revealing deep connections between different states and their automorphisms. Additionally, it explores KMS states and their characterization through modular automorphisms, contributing significantly to the understanding of the structure of type III factors in von Neumann algebras.

The Hiller YH-32 Hornet was a jet-powered helicopter designed in the 1950s as an experimental aircraft. It featured ramjets on its rotor tips, aiming to eliminate the need for a tail rotor. However, the design faced issues with high fuel consumption, poor range, and noise, limiting its practical application. Evaluated by both the U.S. Army and Navy, the YH-32 showed potential as an armed helicopter and for air rescue and reconnaissance roles but ultimately failed to gain widespread adoption due to its limitations. Only a few prototypes were built, and the project was eventually canceled.

Long-chain-alcohol oxidase (EC 1.1.3.20) is an enzyme that oxidizes long-chain alcohols to aldehydes and then to carboxylic acids, playing a crucial role in the omega-oxidation of long-chain alkanes in yeast. It consists of an octamer of 46kD subunits and is bound to the endoplasmic reticulum membrane. This enzyme is involved in the production of dicarboxylic acids for industrial applications, particularly in the synthesis of fragrances, polyamides, and antibiotics.

*Furcifer tuzetae* is a chameleon found in Madagascar. It was first described in 1972, and the specific name, *tuzetae*, honors French parasitologist Odette Tuzet. The International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN) has rated the species as "data deficient" due to a lack of information. It is suspected that the population is declining.

Juini Booth (February 12, 1948 – July 11, 2021) was an American jazz bassist from Buffalo, New York. He began playing piano at age eight and switched to double-bass and electric bass. Booth played with numerous notable musicians including Eddie Harris, Art Blakey, and Sun Ra, and was a long-serving member of Sun Ra's Arkestra.

The Alliance française is a global network of French language and cultural centers founded in Paris in 1883 by prominent figures including Louis Pasteur, Ferdinand de Lesseps, and Jules Verne. With over 440,000 students worldwide, it promotes French language education through independently run local franchises. The Paris headquarters, Alliance française Paris Île-de-France, is the oldest and largest branch, hosting thousands of international students annually. While primarily funded by course fees, it receives partial government support. The organization collaborates with ministries, businesses, and cultural institutions to advance French language learning.

Supersingular Isogeny Key Exchange (SIDH) is a post-quantum cryptographic key exchange protocol based on the mathematics of supersingular elliptic curves and isogenies. Introduced in 2011 by De Feo, Jao, and Plût, SIDH provides forward secrecy and resists attacks from both classical and quantum computers. Unlike traditional Diffie-Hellman, SIDH relies on computing isogenies (rational maps between elliptic curves) rather than discrete logarithms, making it secure against Shor's algorithm.  

The protocol involves two parties exchanging public keys derived from secret isogenies, allowing them to compute a shared secret (the j-invariant of a resulting elliptic curve). SIDH was initially believed to offer strong security, with classical and quantum attack complexities of \(O(p^{1/4})\) and \(O(p^{1/6})\), respectively. However, in 2022, Castryck and Decru developed an efficient key-recovery attack exploiting auxiliary points in SIDH public keys, breaking proposed instantiations like SIKE.  

Despite this vulnerability, SIDH remains notable for its compact key sizes (~564 bytes) and efficient implementations, including constant-time software by Microsoft. It has influenced research in isogeny-based cryptography, including digital signatures and designated-verifier schemes.

Han Lao Da (born Ann Jong Juan, October 22, 1947) is a Singaporean xiangsheng performer, playwright, and the principal of Han Language Centre. He began composing xiangsheng plays in the 1970s and has since worked to promote the art form in Singapore, publishing several collections of scripts.

Wafa Mustafa (born in Masyaf, Syria) is a Syrian journalist and activist known for her advocacy for detainees and missing persons in Syria, including her father, Ali Mustafa, who was forcibly disappeared in 2013. After participating in early anti-government protests, she was arrested in 2011 and later fled to Turkey before settling in Germany. She graduated from Bard College Berlin in 2020. Mustafa has campaigned internationally, including at the UN Security Council and the Al Khatib trial in Germany, where she highlighted human rights abuses by the Syrian regime. She co-founded *Families for Freedom*, a movement demanding justice for Syria's disappeared.

Ivy Winters (born Dustin Winters, October 5, 1986) is an American drag performer, singer, and actor, best known for competing on season five of *RuPaul's Drag Race*, where he was crowned Miss Congeniality. Before his drag career, Winters worked as a circus performer, mastering skills like juggling, stilt-walking, and fire-breathing. Post-*Drag Race*, he hosted the DIY webshow *Queen Acres*, released music, and appeared in documentaries and media projects. Winters married Keith Book in 2015.

The Western Tapes, 1983 is an EP by American country rock band Lone Justice, released in 2018 by Omnivore Recordings. Recorded in 1983 at the Record Plant and Mystic Sound studios in Los Angeles, the six-track EP features early demo versions of songs later re-recorded for their 1985 debut album. Produced by Marvin Etzioni and Ryan Hedgecock, the recordings capture the band's raw energy and blend of country, rock, and punk influences. Critics praised the EP for its authenticity and Maria McKee's powerful vocals, with some noting that these versions surpassed later studio takes. The release followed *The Vaught Tapes, 1983* (2014) and preceded *Live at the Palomino, 1983* (2019).

Kooralbyn International School, established in 1985 in Kooralbyn, Queensland, Australia, is a non-denominational, co-educational P-12 school. Relocated in the mid-1990s due to flooding, the school reopened in 2004 with new leadership and financial backing. It operates on a maroon, grey, and white color scheme and is known for its motto, "Lang-la" (Believe in yourself).

Retaj Hotels & Hospitality is a Qatar-based hotel and hospitality company owned by five Qatari investors. It operates six hotels in Doha, Qatar, under various brands, including Retaj Royale and Retaj Al Rayyan. The company has expanded internationally, with properties in Turkey, Saudi Arabia, and the Comoros, and plans to enter the European and U.S. markets. Retaj's portfolio includes luxury, mid-range, and budget hotels, with significant investments planned for Turkey and other regions.

Muthoot Microfin Limited is a non-banking financial company (NBFC-MFI) headquartered in Mumbai, India. Established in 2012, it is part of the Muthoot Pappachan Group and focuses on microfinance services.

*Freak Magnet* is the seventh studio album by Violent Femmes, released on February 22, 2000. The album was produced by Warren Bruleigh, Violent Femmes, Pierre Henry, and Tom Grimley. Reviews were mixed, with some critics noting a return to form and others finding the album uninspired.

The Slutsk uprising (November 27 – December 31, 1920) was an armed rebellion in Belarus against Soviet rule during the Russian Civil War. Led by local Belarusian nationalists, the uprising sought to defend the independence of the Belarusian Democratic Republic following the Soviet-Polish War. Despite initial successes, the poorly equipped Belarusian forces were defeated by the Red Army. The event remains a symbol of resistance in Belarusian nationalist memory, though it is not officially recognized by the Belarusian government.

Anne Ebbs (born 1940) is an Irish Paralympic athlete and sports administrator, known for her achievements in table tennis, including winning multiple medals at the Paralympic Games from 1972 to 1984. She also played a significant role in sports development, founding the Paralympic Council of Ireland in 1987.

Georgia Douglas Johnson (1880–1966) was an American poet, playwright, and composer. One of the earliest African-American female playwrights, she was part of the Harlem Renaissance, the anti-lynching movement, and the S Street Salon. Atlanta University awarded her an honorary doctorate in literature in 1965.

Jean-Philippe Mateta, born on June 28, 1997, in Sevran, France, is a Congolese-French striker who has played for teams including Châteauroux, Lyon, Le Havre, Mainz 05, and Crystal Palace. He currently plays for Crystal Palace, where he made his debut in 2021.

Matt Johnson (born 15 August 1961) is an English singer, songwriter, and producer, best known as the frontman of the alternative rock band The The. Emerging in 1979, he has released several acclaimed albums, including "Soul Mining" and "Infected." Johnson's work often reflects personal themes, notably influenced by the loss of his brother Eugene. After a 15-year hiatus, he returned in 2017 with new music and a tour, continuing to shape the post-punk and new wave genres.

The 2017 Ole Miss Rebels football team represented the University of Mississippi in the 2017 NCAA Division I FBS football season. Led by interim head coach Matt Luke, the Rebels competed in the Southeastern Conference (SEC) and finished with a 6–6 record (3–5 SEC). Due to NCAA sanctions, the team was ineligible for postseason play. The season was overshadowed by an ongoing NCAA investigation, which resulted in self-imposed penalties, including scholarship reductions and recruiting restrictions. Ole Miss played home games at Vaught–Hemingway Stadium and faced SEC opponents such as Alabama, LSU, and Mississippi State, along with non-conference matchups against California and South Alabama. The Rebels' coaching staff saw significant changes, including the hiring of defensive coordinator Wesley McGriff.

Homaro Cantu Jr. (September 23, 1976 – April 14, 2015) was an American chef and inventor known for his innovative and experimental approach to molecular gastronomy. He gained prominence as the chef of Moto, a Chicago restaurant that featured dishes such as edible menus, carbonated fruit, and food cooked with lasers. Cantu's work at Moto earned him a Michelin star in 2012 and established him as a leading figure in Chicago's innovative food scene.

Beyond his culinary creations, Cantu was a prolific inventor, holding patents for new utensils, cooking devices, and an edible printer. He was also a media personality, appearing on shows like *Iron Chef America* and *Future Food*. Cantu was a passionate advocate for using food to address global issues, exploring the potential of "miracle berries" and edible paper to combat hunger. In 2013, he founded the Trotter Project, a non-profit providing culinary education to underprivileged students. Cantu also opened a coffee house called Berrista focused around the same concept. At the time of his death, he was preparing to open a brewery/brewpub called Crooked Fork. Cantu died by suicide in 2015.

"Wild Wild Life" is a single by Talking Heads from the album True Stories, released in 1986. Known for its quirky new wave synth-rock style and winning a Best Group Video award at the MTV Video Music Awards, the song features a cabaret-themed music video with parodies of various artists and actors.

Seneca Falls is a hamlet and census-designated place (CDP) in Seneca County, New York, United States. Located in the Finger Lakes region, it was originally incorporated as a village in 1831 before dissolving in 2011. The community is historically significant as the site of the 1848 Seneca Falls Convention, the first women's rights convention, which launched the women's suffrage movement. The area was once a major milling center, powered by the Seneca River, and later became home to early industrial innovations, including pump manufacturing. Today, it hosts the Women's Rights National Historical Park and the National Women's Hall of Fame. As of the 2020 census, the population was 6,809.

Theresa Park is an American literary agent, producer, and president of Per Capita Productions. After graduating from Harvard Law School, she worked as a corporate attorney before transitioning to literary representation, founding Park Literary and Media in 2005. In 2019, she co-founded Park & Fine Literary and Media. Park's production company, Per Capita Productions, has produced projects such as *After Yang*, *The Expatriates*, and *ROAR*. Her work spans literature, film, and television, collaborating with prominent creators and production companies.

The Catskill Mountain House was a historic hotel built in 1823 atop the Catskill Mountains in New York, offering sweeping views of the Hudson Valley. A popular destination for artists, writers, and the wealthy, it played a key role in the Hudson River School art movement. Declining in prominence by the 20th century, it closed in 1941 and was demolished in 1963.

Contour crafting is a 3D printing technology developed by Nader Khoshnevis that aims to revolutionize construction. Initially funded by Caterpillar Inc. in 2008, the technology was commercialized by the ACASA project in 2009. Khoshnevis claimed in 2010 that his system could build a complete home in a single day, significantly reducing construction waste and environmental impact. NASA evaluated Contour Crafting for potential use in constructing bases on Mars and the Moon, leading to a 2013 study at the University of Southern California. In 2017, Contour Crafting Corporation, led by Khoshnevis, partnered with Doka Ventures, aiming to deliver the first printers early the following year.

The Department of Social Security (DSS) was an Australian government agency established on December 19, 1972, to manage social security payments and services. It succeeded the Department of Social Services and was dissolved on October 21, 1998, when its functions were transferred to the Department of Family and Community Services.

The Northampton Red Sox were a minor league baseball team based in Cape Charles, Virginia, that played in the Class D Eastern Shore League from 1927 to 1928. They won the league title in 1928 but folded after the season.
The "Alternative Christmas message" is an annual broadcast by Channel 4 since 1993, offering a counterpoint to the Queen's Christmas message.
Newman O'Neal was the mayor of Hobson City, Alabama, until death threats forced him into exile in July 1919.

Menodora is a genus of flowering plants in the family Oleaceae, characterized by bisexual flowers with united calyxes and bright yellow or white corollas. The genus exhibits a distinctive disjunct distribution across the Americas and Africa, suggesting historical biogeographical connections.

The Newmarket Viaduct was opened on 3 September 1966 as New Zealand's first pair of balanced cantilever bridges. Due to earthquake vulnerability and increasing traffic, it was replaced between 2007 and 2013.

WiQuest Communications was a private computer hardware company founded in 2003 in Allen, Texas, that specialized in Wireless USB products. At its peak in early 2008, it held an 85% market share but ceased operations on October 31, 2008, due to various industry challenges.

Matthew Benedict Walker MBE (born 25 April 1978) is a British Paralympic swimmer who has won 11 Paralympic medals, including three golds in relay events, across four Games from 2000 to 2012. Competing in the S7 classification, he also earned numerous medals at World, European, and Commonwealth Championships. Walker was appointed Member of the Order of the British Empire (MBE) in 2009 for his contributions to disabled sport.

The New Zealand Superstock Championship, inaugurated in 1965, is a popular form of dirt track racing in New Zealand. It features full-contact, high-speed races on oval tracks with concrete walls. The championship allows drivers to push each other into the infield, up the wall, or into other cars. Current rules stipulate engines must be no larger than 248 cubic inches, producing around 500 horsepower. With over 132 drivers competing (as of 2021), the series showcases intense and strategic racing.

Kfar Dounin is a village in the Nabatieh Governorate of Lebanon, located at an elevation of 580 meters. Historically, it has a significant Metawileh population and features remnants of a demolished church. The village has been the site of notable events, including a 1986 incident involving UNIFIL and Israeli soldiers.
Diana Nemiroff is a Canadian curator and writer, known for her exhibitions on Indigenous and contemporary art, and a recipient of the Governor General's Award.

Mr. Muthafuckin' eXquire (born Hugh Anthony Allison in 1986) is an American rapper from Brooklyn, New York. Rising to prominence with his 2011 mixtape *Lost in Translation* and its viral single "Huzzah," he gained critical acclaim for his raw lyricism and collaborations with artists like Danny Brown and El-P. After signing with Universal Republic Records in 2012, he released the *Power & Passion* EP but was later dropped. His independent projects include *Kismet* (2013), *Live Forever* (2015), and his self-titled debut album (2019). Known for his unorthodox style, eXquire cites DMX as an influence, reflected in his stage name.

Peter von Hess (29 July 1792 – 4 April 1871) was a German painter known for his battle scenes, landscapes, and depictions of historical events. Trained in Munich, he gained recognition for his works during the Napoleonic Wars and his depictions of the Greek War of Independence, created at the request of King Ludwig I of Bavaria. His notable works include large-scale paintings displayed in Munich's Pinakothek.

La Grosse Décharge Est is a river in the Charlevoix Regional County Municipality of Quebec, Canada, flowing through Baie-Saint-Paul. It originates from a small lake at an elevation of 673 meters (2,208 ft) and runs for 6.5 kilometers (4.0 mi) before joining La Grosse Décharge Ouest, a tributary of the Rivière des Mares and ultimately the Gouffre River. The river descends 249 meters (817 ft) over its course, with surrounding peaks reaching up to 971 meters (3,186 ft). Its name, formalized in 1997 by the Commission de toponymie du Québec, first appeared on a 1955 forest map. The watershed contributes to the Saint Lawrence River via the Gouffre River.

Utsayantha Mountain is a 3,209-foot (978 m) peak in the Catskill Mountains of New York, located east-southeast of Stamford in Delaware County. Named after Utsayantha, the daughter of Lenape Chief Ubiwacha, the mountain is associated with a local legend involving her tragic death. In 1889, a private landowner built an observation house on the summit, later deeded to the town as a public park. A 59-foot (18 m) fire tower, constructed in 1934 by the Civilian Conservation Corps, operated until 1989. After restoration efforts in 2005, the tower reopened to the public and was added to the National Historic Lookout Register in 2015. The mountain remains a notable landmark in the region.

*Ajantrik* is a 1958 Indian Bengali-language film directed by Ritwik Ghatak, based on a short story by Subodh Ghosh. The film follows Bimal, a lonely taxi driver in a small industrial town, and his deep attachment to his dilapidated 1920 Chevrolet taxi, Jagaddal. Through episodic storytelling, it explores themes of human connection, industrialization, and modernity. 

The film received international attention, including at the Venice Film Festival, where it captivated audiences despite the lack of subtitles. Critics have praised its portrayal of the conflict between nature and mechanized civilization, with poet Alokeranjan Dasgupta describing it as a unique contribution to modernist cinema.
Robert Henry Doolan (1917–2022) was a World War II veteran and prisoner of war who served as a navigator in the Army Air Corps, participating in 12 combat missions before being captured.

Owain Yeoman (born 2 July 1978) is a Welsh actor known for his roles as Wayne Rigsby in *The Mentalist* (2008–2014) and General Benedict Arnold in *Turn: Washington's Spies* (2015–2017). He has also appeared in films such as *Troy* (2004) and *American Sniper* (2014).

Prakash Nanjappa (born February 29, 1976) is an Indian shooter. He won a bronze medal in the 10 metre air pistol event at the 2013 ISSF World Cup in Changwon. At the 2014 Commonwealth Games in Glasgow, he won a silver medal in the 10 m air pistol event. Nanjappa qualified for the 2016 Rio Olympics in the Men's 50 metres Pistol event.

"Maximum Carnage" is a superhero crossover event published by Marvel Comics from May to August 1993. It features a killing spree by Carnage and his followers in New York City, involving various Spider-Man characters and other superheroes. The event culminates in the heroes' victory over Carnage, who fakes his own death.

A. J. Alan, born Leslie Harrison Lambert on November 11, 1883, was an English magician, intelligence officer, author, and radio broadcaster. He gained fame for his whimsical storytelling on the radio, becoming a popular figure in the 1920s and 1930s, while also serving in naval intelligence during both World Wars.

Breac Maodhóg is an 11th-century Irish house-shaped reliquary, likely used as a battle standard, associated with St. Maedoc of Ferns. Made of bronze on a wooden base, it features finely detailed figures of saints, clerics, and apostles, with expressive faces and elaborate robes. One notable figure depicts the oldest known visual representation of a harp. Originally linked to Drumlane monastery in County Cavan, it was later associated with Rossinver, County Leitrim, before being stolen in the 19th century. The shrine, now in poor condition, is housed in the National Museum of Ireland. Its figures, resembling those in the *Soiscél Molaisse*, are praised for their lifelike humanity and intricate craftsmanship.
Kleiner Tiergarten is a historic urban park in Berlin's Moabit.

Panhard was a French automobile and military vehicle manufacturer founded in 1887 by René Panhard and Émile Levassor. Initially known as Panhard et Levassor, the company pioneered automotive innovations, including the modern transmission and the standard car layout. It produced passenger cars until 1967, after which it focused solely on military vehicles. Acquired by Citroën and later Renault Trucks Defense, Panhard ceased operations in 2018. Its legacy includes the Panhard rod suspension system.

The Sabre Wildcat is a Canadian two-seat, high-wing, weight-shift control ultralight aircraft that was designed and produced by Sabre Aircraft. The aircraft features a cable-braced wing covered in Dacron, an open cockpit, tricycle landing gear, and a pusher engine.

Nikaho (にかほ市) is a city located in Akita Prefecture, Japan, bordered by the Sea of Japan and Yamagata Prefecture. Established on October 1, 2005, through the merger of several towns, Nikaho has a population of approximately 24,291 as of December 2019. The city features a humid subtropical climate and is known for its agricultural and fishing industries, as well as Mount Chōkai, a popular destination for outdoor activities.

Cipriano Piccolpasso (1524–1579) was an Italian Renaissance polymath, known for his contributions as a surveyor, civil and military engineer, draughtsman, poet, and writer. He was a member of the literary Accademia degli Eccentrici in Perugia and co-founded the Accademia del Disegno in 1573, one of the earliest academies for Italian artists.
