In 2001, she and her wife, Diane Olson, successfully sued California for marriage rights, becoming one of the first legally married lesbian couples in the state. Tyler also co-founded the comedy duo Harrison and Tyler and produced trans-inclusive women's music festivals. Her activism includes campaigns against homophobic media and nationwide protests for LGBTQ+ rights.

ESET NOD32 Antivirus is an antivirus software suite created by the Slovak company ESET. First released in 1987, NOD32 was originally named NOD-ICE and was DOS-based. ESET also offers ESET Smart Security, ESET Mobile Security, ESET Remote Administrator and ESET SysInspector, among other programs.

The Subnetwork Access Protocol (SNAP) is a mechanism for multiplexing protocols on networks using IEEE 802.2 Logical Link Control (LLC). It allows more protocols than can be distinguished by the 8-bit 802.2 Service Access Point (SAP) fields. SNAP supports identifying protocols by Ethernet type field values and vendor-private protocol identifier spaces. It is used with IEEE 802.3, IEEE 802.11 and other IEEE 802 physical network layers, as well as with non-IEEE 802 physical network layers such as FDDI that use 802.2 LLC. The 5-octet SNAP header follows the 802.2 LLC header if the destination SAP (DSAP) and the source SAP (SSAP) contain hexadecimal values of AA or AB.
The Botswana National Stadium is a multi-purpose stadium in Gaborone, Botswana, with a capacity of 25,000. It is home to the Botswana national football team.

*Rugby League Week* was an Australian sports magazine dedicated to rugby league, published weekly during the NRL season from 1970 to 2017. Owned by Bauer Media Group, it featured news, player profiles, match previews, and ratings. The magazine introduced the "Player of the Year" award and created "The Immortals," honoring rugby league's greatest players.

(1004) Belopolskya, discovered by S. Belyavskyj on September 5, 1923, at Simeiz Observatory, orbits the Sun in the Cybele asteroid family and is named after astrophysicist Aristarkh Belopolsky. It has a diameter of approximately 79.83 km, an albedo of 0.0348, and a rotation period of about 9.44 hours.

Homaro Cantu Jr. (September 23, 1976 – April 14, 2015) was an American chef and innovator known for his work in molecular gastronomy. Born in Tacoma, Washington, Cantu developed a passion for science and engineering early in life. He attended the Western Culinary Institute and worked as a sous chef for Charlie Trotter before founding Moto, a restaurant noted for its experimental cuisine. Moto earned a Michelin star, and Cantu also opened iNG, focusing on "flavor-tripping" with the miracle berry. His culinary career was marked by groundbreaking inventions and a commitment to ending world hunger through innovative food solutions. Cantu's legacy includes his role in establishing Chicago as a hub for innovative cuisine and his advocacy for culinary education in underserved communities. He died in 2015, leaving behind a significant impact on the culinary world.

Christian Poncelet (24 March 1928 – 11 September 2020) served as President of the French Senate from 2 October 1998 to 30 September 2008, succeeding René Monory. A member of the UMP political party, Poncelet was first elected to the National Assembly in 1962 and to the Senate in 1977, serving multiple terms. He was awarded the Grand Cross of the Royal Norwegian Order of Merit in 2000. Poncelet passed away in Remiremont at the age of 92.
Konstantin Ivanov Slavchev (born 17 July 1976), most famously known as Konstantin, is a Bulgarian pop-folk singer. His career began in 1999 with "Cherna roza".

Nomads Rugby Football Club, founded in 1989, is based in Hong Kong and competes in the G4S League Division 2, 5, and 7. Led by Steve Jones (coach) and Craig Wilson (captain), the club has seen success in recent seasons, including third-place finishes and league wins. They have partnered with the Flying Kukris RFC to develop youth rugby talent.
Pottawattamie Park is a town in LaPorte County, Indiana, United States. Incorporated in 1936, it is entirely surrounded by Michigan City and had a population of 242 as of the 2020 census.

Hopatcong State Park is a 163-acre (0.66 km²) public recreation area located in Roxbury Township, New Jersey, on the shores of Lake Hopatcong. Established in 1922, the park offers swimming, fishing, boating, and winter activities like ice fishing and sledding. It features picnic areas, playgrounds, sports courts, and a boat ramp. The park also preserves historical elements, including the Lake Hopatcong Historical Museum, housed in a former Morris Canal lock tender’s house, and a display of canal technology, including a Scotch Turbine from the Morris Canal. Operated by the New Jersey Division of Parks and Forestry, it is a popular destination for outdoor recreation.

James R. Jordan Sr. (July 31, 1936 – July 23, 1993) was an American mechanic and the father of basketball legend Michael Jordan. He was murdered in 1993 during a carjacking, a crime that gained national attention. His killers were convicted and sentenced to life in prison.

Bill Long (1932–2010) was an Irish writer and broadcaster born in Waterford. He worked as a journalist for various Irish newspapers and at Revlon, befriended Raymond Chandler, and wrote numerous books, including "Bright Light, White Water" and "Change of Heart."

The Associated Equipment Company (AEC) was founded in 1912 and became a major manufacturer of lorries and buses, producing iconic models like the Regent and Mammoth. AEC operated until 1979, when it was closed by British Leyland. The company's vehicles were widely used in the UK and exported globally, including to Latin America, Spain, and Portugal. AEC's legacy includes numerous successful commercial vehicle models and significant contributions to British transportation history.

The Black Country derby is the name given to football matches between West Bromwich Albion and Wolverhampton Wanderers, the two major clubs in the Black Country. With both clubs being founder members of the Football League in 1888, the derby is one of the oldest in league football. The first match was played in 1883, with Albion winning 4–2.

*Saint Overboard* is a mystery novel by Leslie Charteris, published in 1936. It is part of *The Saint* series. The plot sees Simon Templar, alias The Saint, rescuing a private detective, Loretta Page, from modern-day pirates led by Kurt Vogel, who are stealing sunken treasure. The Saint is assisted by colleagues Roger Conway and Peter Quentin, and his manservant Orace. The film rights were purchased by RKO Studios, but no film was produced. A radio play adaptation was broadcast in 1995.

Gorky Park, formerly known as Gorodskoy Garden, is a municipal park in Rostov-on-Don, Russia. Established in 1813, the park spans 12.2 hectares (30 acres). The park was created from a former town dump and dirty stream, through the efforts of private organizations. Over time, several gardens were combined to form the urban park, divided into Upper and Lower sections. The park features walking tracks, seven fountains, playgrounds, amusement rides, and sculptures. Indoor attractions include an observatory and monuments to Lenin and the Great October Revolution. An open-air gallery hosts art expositions.

Hristo Chernopeev (c. 1868 – November 6, 1915) was a Bulgarian Army sergeant, IMRO revolutionary, and politician. He participated in the Miss Stone Affair and was involved in the Ilinden Uprising and the Balkan Wars. He died in World War I.

Larry Keel, born on November 25, 1968, in Manassas, Virginia, is a renowned bluegrass musician, guitarist, and singer who has been active since 1975. Keel, along with his wife Jenny, formed the band Larry Keel & Natural Bridge, known for their adventurous and innovative bluegrass sound. He has also been a member of various other bands, including Magraw Gap and the Larry Keel Experience. Keel is celebrated for his collaborations with notable artists and his involvement in major festivals across the country. His musical style blends traditional bluegrass with progressive elements, earning him recognition both nationally and internationally.

The Swedish Society for Anthropology and Geography (SSAG) is a scholarly organization dedicated to the fields of anthropology and geography. Established in 1878, the society is known for its prestigious awards, including the Vega Medal, created in 1881 to honor Adolf Erik Nordenskiöld's discovery of the Northeast Passage. The medal is awarded approximately every three years to an outstanding physical geographer. The society also awards the SSAG Medal, previously named the Anders Retzius Medal, which was renamed in 2015 due to Retzius's controversial racial studies. These awards are presented annually by the King of Sweden on April 24.

The society also publishes *Kritisk Etnografi* (Critical Ethnography), a biannual, peer-reviewed, open-access journal launched in 2018, focusing on anthropology and ethnography in collaboration with Uppsala University's DiVA archive.

*Hallelujah, Baby!* is a 1967 Broadway musical with music by Jule Styne, lyrics by Adolph Green and Betty Comden, and a book by Arthur Laurents. The story follows Georgina, an ambitious African American woman navigating racism and societal challenges as she rises to stardom through the Great Depression, World War II, and the civil rights movement. The original production won five Tony Awards, including Best Musical.

"Alice – A Fight For Life" is a 1982 documentary film directed and narrated by John Willis, focusing on the health impacts of asbestos exposure. Produced by Yorkshire Television, the film runs for 94.5 minutes and aired on ITV. It emerged from a context of growing public concern over asbestos-related health issues, particularly following critical reports on working conditions at the Cape plant at Acre Mill. The documentary was motivated by dissatisfaction with the findings of the Simpson Committee, which was perceived to favor the asbestos industry. Willis, alongside researchers James Cutler and Peter Moore, aimed to expose the deceptive practices surrounding asbestos safety and advocate for better working conditions.

Villa Park is a neighborhood in West Denver, located about two to three miles west of Downtown Denver. Characterized by a diverse population and hilly terrain, it features several parks, creeks, and a mix of residential and commercial development.

Stephanie Zimmermann (October 29, 1973 – November 10, 2020) was a German physicist known for her contributions to high-energy particle physics, particularly within the ATLAS experiment at CERN. She earned her Master's and Ph.D. degrees from the University of Freiburg, where she focused on the muon system of the Large Hadron Collider. Zimmermann served as the ATLAS Run-Coordinator and led the New Small Wheel project, which aimed to upgrade the muon detection capabilities of the ATLAS Muon System. She received several awards for her academic work, including the Marc Virchaux Prize for her doctoral thesis.

Pa amb tomàquet is a popular Catalan tapas dish originating in the 1800s, consisting of bread topped with tomato, olive oil, and sometimes garlic and salt. It is commonly found in Catalonia, the Balearic Islands, and Aragon. The dish can be pre-made or prepared by the diner. In Majorca, a specific variety of tomato called Tomàtiga de Ramellet is used. Traditionally, the base is made with toasted slices of pa de pagès, a round loaf of wheat bread. The dish is often served with sausages, ham, cheese, or grilled vegetables. While the exact origin is disputed, it is widely regarded as a symbol of Catalan cuisine and identity.

"Let It Go" is a jazz canto-jazz song by Jacky Cheung from his album Private Corner, released in April 2010. Written by Daniel Lindstrom, Daniele Musto, and Roxanne Seeman, and produced by Andrew Tuason, the song features Cantonese lyrics adapted by Kenny So. It reached number 16 on the HMVHK sales chart and was performed by Cheung in concerts and tours.

Nerima Wako-Ojiwa (born 1989) is a Kenyan political analyst and corporate executive. She is the executive director of Siasa Place, a youth-led political organization she co-founded in 2015. Educated at Jacksonville State University, she holds degrees in journalism, sociology, and public administration.

The Newmarket Viaduct is a motorway bridge in Auckland, New Zealand, originally opened in 1966 as the country's first balanced cantilever bridge. Due to seismic vulnerabilities and increasing traffic, it was replaced between 2007 and 2013 with a stronger, four-lane structure designed to withstand major earthquakes.

Prakash Vir Shastri (1923–1977) was an Indian politician and scholar who served as a Member of Parliament in both the Lok Sabha and Rajya Sabha from 1958 to 1977. He was known for opposing English as the national language and advocating for Hindi, and for proposing the Religious Protection Bill in 1960. Shastri was killed in a train accident in 1977.

The Vladimir Lenin monument is located in Azov, Rostov Oblast, Russia. It was opened on April 30, 1978. The monument's authors were Yuri Orekhov, Vladimir Peterburzhtsev and Alexander Stepanov. The total height of the monument is 10.8 meters.
St John's Rectory in Chester, built around 1750 as the rectory for St John's Church, is a Georgian-style Grade II* listed building located at 3 Vicar's Lane, designated in 1955.

Joanne M. Garvey is an American attorney. She grew up in Oakland, California, and received a B.A. from the University of California at Berkeley and a J.D. from UC Berkeley School of Law, where she was one of only five women in her graduating class.

Garvey began her legal career in Santa Barbara, California, in 1962. She then spent 25 years at the law firm Jordan, Keeler and Seligman in San Francisco. In 1988, she joined Heller Ehrman, also in San Francisco, and subsequently moved to Sheppard, Mullin, Richter & Hampton after Heller Ehrman dissolved in 2008.

Garvey has received several notable awards, including the State Bar of California Taxation Section Joanne M. Garvey Award, named in her honor, and the American Bar Association Commission on Women's 2003 Margaret Brent Women Lawyers of Achievement Award.

The Pearl Carpet of Baroda, commissioned in 1865 by the Maharaja of Baroda, features pearls, diamonds, rubies, and emeralds on deerskin and silk. Currently in the National Museum of Qatar, it measures 264 cm long and 173 cm wide.

Manuel Sager (born 1955) is a Swiss diplomat and lawyer who served as the Swiss Ambassador to the United States (2010–2014) and Director of the Swiss Agency for Development and Cooperation (2014–2020).

Pheona Wall is a Ugandan lawyer and student leader, known for her roles in the Uganda Law Society, where she was elected president in 2020. She holds degrees from Makerere University and has a background in public relations and community advocacy.
Roy Oxlade (1929–2014) was an English painter known for his expressive, metaphorical works and his writings on modernist painting. He studied under David Bomberg and later taught at Goldsmiths.

"Sendiri" is a 1984 studio album by Indonesian pop singer Chrisye, marking a departure from his previous art rock style. Produced by Addie MS, it features the titular track and other singles, winning the BASF Award for best-selling album.

*The 1900 House* is a historical reenactment reality television series that originally aired on Channel 4 in the United Kingdom and PBS in the United States from December 28, 1999, to July 3, 2000. The series follows the Bowler family as they live in a restored 1890s terraced house in Charlton, South-East London, modified to reflect the conditions of the year 1900. Modern amenities were removed, and period-appropriate features, such as gas lighting and a cast-iron oven, were installed. The family faced challenges adapting to the era's lifestyle, including hiring and dismissing a maid. The series, which featured historian Daru Rooke as a consultant, inspired several sequels and was later released on VHS and DVD.

Benedicto "Bong" Ducat (born May 6, 1957) is a Filipino painter and art professor known for his works such as *The Joyful Moods of Nature*, *Forest Leaves*, and *Lotus*. A graduate of the University of Santo Tomas College of Fine Arts, he has held various roles in art education and craftsmanship and currently teaches at his alma mater.

The England Boxing National Amateur Championships, formerly known as the ABA Championships, is an annual boxing competition inaugurated in 1881. Initially held in four weight categories, the event has evolved to include eleven categories as of 2022. The Championships are organized by England Boxing and serve as a platform for selecting representatives for the Four Nations Championship.

*Jumping Flash! 2* is a first-person platform game developed by Exact and MuuMuu and published by Sony Computer Entertainment for the PlayStation. Released in 1996, it is the sequel to *Jumping Flash!* and retains much of its predecessor's gameplay, featuring a first-person perspective, triple-jump mechanics, and projectile-based combat. Players control Robbit, a robotic rabbit, as he rescues the inhabitants of Little Muu from the alien invader Captain Kabuki, who teams up with Robbit's former foe, Baron Aloha. The game introduces new features, including rescuing MuuMuus, a revamped interface, power-ups, and collectible performance medals.

The game was praised for its improved graphics, larger levels, and expanded features, though critics noted its short length and low difficulty. It was lauded for its innovative gameplay and visual design, with *Electronic Gaming Monthly* ranking it among the "100 Best Games of All Time" in 1997. The soundtrack, composed by Takeo Miratsu, was released in Japan alongside the game. *Jumping Flash! 2* was re-released on the PlayStation Network in 2009, maintaining its original gameplay and controls. Despite being considered more of an expansion than a groundbreaking sequel, it remains a notable entry in the early 3D platforming genre.

Andrée Geulen (6 September 1921 – 31 May 2022) was a Belgian teacher and member of the Committee for the Defence of Jews (CDJ) during the Holocaust in Belgium. As one of the few non-Jewish members of the CDJ, she persuaded Jewish families to give their children to the organization, which then placed them with non-Jewish families, schools, and religious institutions under false identities. She was personally involved in the arrangements made for 300 Jewish children. After the war, she remained involved with the Jewish community. Geulen was recognized as Righteous Among the Nations in 1989 and granted honorary Israeli citizenship in 2007.

The Technicolor Time Machine is a 1967 science fiction novel by Harry Harrison. It follows a struggling film director who, to save his failing studio, uses a time machine to shoot a historical movie in the past—only to accidentally alter history itself. The novel blends humor, time travel paradoxes, and Hollywood satire, culminating in an unexpected twist where fiction becomes reality.
Charles Parker (1809–1898) was a 19th-century New Zealand politician representing Motueka and Massacre Bay.

Osterheide is an unincorporated area in the Heidekreis district of Lower Saxony, Germany, covering 177.99 km² with a population of 4,437 as of 2022. Established in 1945 after the division of the Bergen Area Estate, it includes the NATO Bergen-Hohne training ranges. Its administration is governed by federal regulations for unincorporated areas.
Dame Thea King DBE FRCM FGSM (1925–2007) was a renowned English clarinettist, teacher, and chamber musician.

Margaret Cuninggim (October 15, 1914 – July 4, 1986) was a university professor and administrator born in Nashville, Tennessee. She earned degrees from Duke, Columbia, and Northwestern Universities. Cuninggim taught at several institutions, including the University of Montevallo, Ripon College, and Vanderbilt University, where she served as Dean of Women and later Dean of Student Services. She was honored posthumously with the Margaret Cuninggim Women's Center at Vanderbilt and the Cuninggim Lecture series, and a fellowship through Alpha Lambda Delta.
**Maureen Sullivan** is an American librarian and educator. She was the president of the American Library Association from 2012 to 2013.

Conrad Richardt Strauss (born 29 January 1986) is a retired professional rugby union player who played as a hooker. Born in Pretoria, South Africa, he began his career with the Free State Cheetahs, winning the 2006 Currie Cup and later playing for the Central Cheetahs in Super Rugby. In 2009, he joined Leinster Rugby, where he became a fan favorite and contributed to their Heineken Cup victories, including the 2011 final. Strauss represented Ireland internationally, earning his first cap in 2012 and playing in the 2015 Rugby World Cup. He retired from professional rugby at the end of the 2017–18 season.

The AN/FPS-24 radar is an early warning radar system developed by General Electric, with twelve units built in the United States between 1958 and 1962. Operating in the VHF frequency range, it features a large antenna reflector and has a range of 250 nautical miles. Initial testing revealed design issues, leading to modifications, while deployment exposed bearing problems due to the antenna's weight. Some units were later upgraded for joint surveillance duties, and one installation has been designated as a historic site.

*Paddleton* is a 2019 American drama film directed by Alex Lehmann, starring Mark Duplass and Ray Romano. It premiered at Sundance on February 1, 2019, and was released on Netflix on February 22, 2019. The film centers on two middle-aged men and their unlikely friendship when one of them is diagnosed with terminal cancer.

Paul Alden Brinegar Jr. (December 19, 1917 – March 27, 1995) was an American character actor best known for his roles in Western films and television series. He played "Dog" Kelley in *The Life and Legend of Wyatt Earp* and Wishbone in *Rawhide*.

Thomas Bernard Hackett VC (15 June 1836 – 5 October 1880) was an Irish-born British Army officer and recipient of the Victoria Cross (VC), the highest military decoration for valor in the British armed forces. He was awarded the VC for his bravery during the Indian Mutiny in 1857. Hackett later achieved the rank of lieutenant colonel and served in the Crimean War, Indian Mutiny, and Third Anglo-Ashanti War. He died in a firearm accident in 1880, and his VC is displayed at the Imperial War Museum in London.

The Great Lakes Depression is a large freshwater basin located in Mongolia, covering an area of approximately 100,000 square kilometers (39,000 square miles). It is characterized by a system of interconnected shallow lakes with extensive reed belts, situated within a desert steppe environment. The region is ecologically significant as it supports important wetlands that serve as habitats for rare and migratory bird species, including the Eurasian spoonbill, black stork, osprey, white-tailed eagle, swan goose, and bar-headed goose. A small population of great white pelicans also remains in the area, nesting in regions with abundant fish and vegetation.

The Great Lakes Depression is notable for its unique fish biodiversity, with a high proportion of endemic or near-endemic species. These include members of the genera *Oreoleuciscus* (Altai osmans), *Thymallus* (graylings), and *Triplophysa* (stone loaches), which thrive in the region's aquatic ecosystems.

Ellen Battelle Dietrick (1847 – November 25, 1895) was an American suffragist and advocate for women's rights, known for her leadership in various organizations promoting women's education and civic reform in Kentucky and Massachusetts.

Dragan Aleksić (born May 1, 1970) is a Serbian basketball coach and former player, currently serving as the head coach of Pécsi VSK-Veolia in the Hungarian League. His playing career spanned from 1989 to 2010, during which he played for several teams, including Crvena zvezda and Spartak. Aleksić transitioned to coaching in 2012, holding various positions, including head coach of Soproni and Szolnoki Olaj.

McKenzie is a city in northern Tennessee, United States, located at the intersection of Carroll, Weakley, and Henry counties. Incorporated in 1869, it spans 6.37 square miles and had a population of 5,529 as of the 2020 census.

GABA transporter type 2 (GAT2) is a protein involved in the transport of gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA) and taurine across cell membranes. It is expressed in various tissues, including the brain, liver, and kidneys, though its physiological roles remain partially understood. In the brain, GAT2 facilitates the efflux of GABA and taurine through the blood-brain barrier, but it has a lower affinity for GABA compared to GAT1 and GAT3, which are more influential in neurotransmitter activity. In the liver, GAT2 is primarily involved in taurine transport and may play a role in GABA uptake from the intestine. In the kidneys, it is localized to the basolateral membranes of proximal tubules, though its function is unclear. While GAT2's clinical significance is undetermined, GABA transporters are linked to epilepsy and other neurological disorders.
Les Gandar (1919 – 16 December 1994) was a New Zealand politician, cabinet minister, educator, and diplomat who served as High Commissioner.

Lewis Madison Terman (January 15, 1877 – December 21, 1956) was an American psychologist and professor at Stanford University. He is known for his work in intelligence testing and positive psychology of talent. He revised the Binet-Simon Scale, creating the Stanford-Binet Intelligence Scales, a widely used IQ test. Terman also conducted a longitudinal study of gifted children, known as the "Termites," to challenge negative stereotypes and understand the development of talent. He believed that IQ was largely inherited and advocated for the use of IQ tests in schools to classify children. Terman's work contributed to the field of educational psychology and the understanding of intelligence.

Horse pulling is a competitive sport in which teams of draft horses test their strength by pulling weighted loads, traditionally using a dynamometer. Originating from farmers challenging each other's workhorses, it has evolved into an organized event with standardized rules. Competitions begin with a set weight (often 1,500 pounds), and teams that successfully pull the load advance to higher weights until a winner is determined. Safety regulations, enforced by organizations like the Eastern Draft Horse Association, ensure the well-being of the horses. The sport emphasizes conditioning to prevent injuries, as modern pulling horses are typically bred and trained specifically for competition rather than farm labor.

Living Colour is an American rock band formed in New York City in 1984. The band consists of guitarist Vernon Reid, vocalist Corey Glover, bassist Doug Wimbish, and drummer Will Calhoun. Stylistically, their music is a creative fusion of hard rock, funk metal, and alternative metal.

Living Colour achieved mainstream success in the late 1980s with their debut album, *Vivid*, released in 1988. The album featured the hit single "Cult of Personality," which gained significant exposure on MTV and helped propel the album to #6 on the Billboard 200 chart. The band's second album, *Time's Up* (1990), showcased their musical diversity, incorporating elements of jazz fusion, punk rock, hip hop, and more, winning a Grammy Award for Best Hard Rock Performance.

After releasing their third album, *Stain*, in 1993, Living Colour disbanded in 1995 but reunited in 2000. Since then, they have released several more albums, including *Collideøscope* (2003), *The Chair in the Doorway* (2009), and *Shade* (2017). Living Colour has been praised for their innovative sound and socially conscious lyrics, earning them a place among VH1's 100 Greatest Artists of Hard Rock. "Cult of Personality" has remained a popular song, featured in video games and as the entrance music for professional wrestler CM Punk.

Gracefield is a city in western Quebec, Canada, constituted on March 13, 2002, from the merger of Wright-Gracefield-Northfield. Located in the Outaouais region and part of the La Vallée-de-la-Gatineau Regional County Municipality, it covers an area of 455.40 km² (175.83 sq mi) and had a population of 2,376 in the 2021 Census, down 3.5% from 2016. Known for its history dating back to 1840 and notable events like the 1974 flood and 2010 earthquake, Gracefield has faced challenges and opportunities, including former mica mines and recent zinc mining interests.
Cheick Oumar Dabo (born 1981) is a Malian former footballer who played as a striker.

Brown Boy 2 is a reissue of the EP Brown Boy by Nav, released on May 11, 2020. It includes additional tracks from Nav's second studio album, Bad Habits, and was announced shortly after the release of Good Intentions. The project's title, "Brown Boy," reflects Nav's Indian heritage, and the reissue features new and previously leaked content. The album spans 37 minutes and 40 seconds and was produced by various artists including Nav, 1K Audio, and Jacc Austin. This reissue follows Nav's Good Intentions and precedes Emergency Tsunami in his discography.

FC Emmen, founded on 21 August 1925, is a Dutch football club that reached the Eredivisie for the first time in 2018 after winning the promotion/relegation play-off finals. Based in Emmen, the club plays at De Oude Meerdijk with a capacity of 8,600. Managed by Dick Lukkien, FC Emmen competes in Eredivisie Divisie. Despite a brief stint in the Eredivisie, the club was relegated in 2021.

Sergio Raul Torres (born 11 July 1981) is an Argentinian footballer. Torres began his career in Argentina before moving to England. He played for Molesey and Basingstoke Town before joining Wycombe Wanderers in 2005. He later played for Peterborough United, Lincoln City (on loan), Crawley Town and Whitehawk. He finished his playing career with Eastbourne Borough.

An avulsion injury involves the tearing away of a body part or tissue, commonly affecting the skin, ears, eyelids, nails, and teeth. Skin avulsions, often resulting from accidents, can range from minor flaps to severe amputations. In rock climbing, a specific type of skin avulsion known as a "flapper" occurs on the fingers. Other types include ear and eyelid avulsions, which may require surgical reattachment or reconstruction. Brachial plexus avulsions involve nerve damage, while tooth avulsions can lead to permanent loss if not promptly treated. Surgical avulsions may also be performed to alleviate medical conditions.
Charles Parker (1809–1898) was a New Zealand politician who served multiple terms in Parliament, representing Motueka.

FC Stal Kamianske, founded in 1926, is a Ukrainian football club that has undergone several name changes and reorganizations. The club, originally known as FC Metalist Kamianske, participated in various Soviet and Ukrainian leagues. After merging with FC Metalurh Donetsk in 2015, it took the latter's place in the Ukrainian Premier League. However, the club faced financial difficulties and was eventually relegated, changing its name and location to PFC Feniks Bucha. FC Stal Kamianske's home colors are silver, blue, and white.

*Raman Abdullah* is a 1997 Indian Tamil-language film written and directed by Balu Mahendra. Starring Sivakumar and Karan, the film features music by Ilaiyaraaja. Despite its notable cast and crew, it was a commercial failure.

Bharatpur is an Assembly constituency in Murshidabad district, West Bengal, India, covering areas like Bharatpur II and I community development blocks. It is part of the Baharampur Lok Sabha constituency and has seen various political parties win elections since 1951.
S. P. Singh (born 1948) is an Indian biochemist, professor, and author.

Greeny, a 1959 Gibson Les Paul guitar, has a rich history tied to English musician Peter Green. Green purchased the guitar for sixty guineas and used it extensively with his band Fleetwood Mac, contributing to the recording of several early hits including "Oh Well", "Black Magic Woman", and "Albatross". In 1970, the guitar was passed to Gary Moore, who used it extensively in his career, including on Thin Lizzy's "Black Rose: A Rock Legend" and his solo single "Parisienne Walkways". In 2014, Kirk Hammett of Metallica purchased Greeny and has since used it in both studio recordings and live performances. Known for its unique "out-of-phase" tone, the guitar's distinctive configuration includes PAF pickups and a flipped neck pickup, which has inspired various reproductions and custom models. Greeny has also been the subject of tribute concerts and continues to be celebrated for its iconic sound and historical significance.

Canajoharie Creek is a 69.22-square-mile (179.3 km²) tributary of the Mohawk River in central New York, flowing through Otsego and Montgomery counties. Originating near Cherry Valley, it travels northeast, passing through the Canajoharie Gorge and over the 45-foot (14 m) Canajoharie Falls before joining the Mohawk River at the village of Canajoharie. The creek's name derives from the Mohawk term for "the pot that washes itself," referencing a deep pothole known as the "boiling pot." Historically significant, the area was settled by Europeans in the 18th century and used by General James Clinton's army during the American Revolution. The creek supports brown trout and other fish species, with water quality rated Class C by the DEC.

Hadia Tajik (born 18 July 1983) is a Norwegian politician, lawyer, and journalist who has served as a Member of Parliament for the Labour Party since 2009. She was the youngest minister in Norwegian history and the first Muslim to serve in the cabinet, holding the position of Minister of Culture from 2012 to 2013. She later served as Minister of Labour and Social Inclusion from 2021 to 2022. Tajik has also been the deputy leader of the Labour Party (2015–2022). Born to Pakistani immigrants in Rogaland, she holds degrees in journalism, human rights, and law.

The 1950 World Snooker Championship took place from December 1949 to March 1950 in England. Walter Donaldson of Scotland defeated Fred Davis of England in the final, 51–46, at Tower Circus in Blackpool. The qualifying rounds were held in Battersea, London, from October to November 1949, with Kingsley Kennerley emerging as the winner.

The Sabre Wildcat is a two-seat, weight-shift control ultralight aircraft with a cable-braced high wing and pusher engine. Designed for affordability, it features an open cockpit, tricycle landing gear, and a Dacron-covered wing.

Borscht Corporation is a Miami-based film collective known for its innovative short films and contributions to independent cinema. The organization has garnered significant media attention and has screened its work at prestigious venues, including the Museum of Modern Art and major film festivals like Sundance and Venice. Borscht Corp. hosts a quasi-annual film festival in Miami, recognized as one of the coolest and most unconventional film festivals globally. The collective has received numerous accolades, including grants and awards for its filmmakers' works.

"Tsunagu" is a pop rock single by Japanese boy band Arashi, released on June 28, 2017, under J Storm. It served as the theme song for the film *Shinobi no Kuni*, starring member Satoshi Ohno. The single debuted at number one on both the Oricon and Billboard Japan charts, selling over 389,000 copies in its first week and earning a platinum certification. It was released in regular and limited editions, each featuring different B-sides and bonus content.

Andrée Geulen (6 September 1921 – 31 May 2022) was a Belgian teacher and member of the resistance during World War II, known for her work with the Committee for the Defence of Jews (CDJ) in Belgium. She helped rescue and hide around 300 Jewish children, placing them with Catholic families and institutions under false identities to protect them from Nazi persecution. After the war, she worked to reunite hidden children with their families and supported Jewish survivors. Recognized as Righteous Among the Nations in 1989, she received honorary Israeli citizenship in 2007. Geulen remained an advocate for pacifist and anti-racist causes throughout her life.

Estelle Desanges, born in Bordeaux, France in 1977, is a French pornographic actress who began her career in 1999 and gained recognition for her work, including winning awards for Best French New Starlet and Best European Supporting Actress. She has since transitioned into marketing and hosting, advocating for safer practices in the adult film industry.

Suzanne M. Levine (born June 28, 1957) is an American podiatric physician known for her innovative aesthetic podiatric treatments and her media presence as a foot care expert. She is the founder of the Institute Beauté and has appeared on numerous television programs, including NBC's The Today Show and The Dr. Oz Show. Levine is also an author of several books on foot health, including *My Feet Are Killing Me!* and has participated in humanitarian efforts, donating shoes to communities in need.

Aristides Aquino, born on April 22, 1994, in Santo Domingo, Dominican Republic, is a right-handed outfielder who debuted with the Cincinnati Reds in 2018. He has played for multiple teams, including the Reds, Louisville Bats, and Chunichi Dragons, and is known for his strong performance in the minors, including hitting seven home runs in his first ten major league games in 2019.

The year 1945 saw the selection of All-America teams in college football. The NCAA recognizes 10 published All-American teams as "official" designations for consensus determinations.

During the 1940s, African-American players were often excluded from major college football programs and played at Historically Black Colleges and Universities (HBCUs). While major All-America selectors did not include players from HBCUs, *The Pittsburgh Courier* selected its own All-America team from players at those institutions.

CALSTAR is a nonprofit air medical transport service based in McClellan, California. Founded in 1983, it operates helicopters and fixed-wing aircraft across Northern California, Central California, and Northern Nevada. Acquired by REACH in 2016, it is now part of Global Medical Response.

Ellis Bitter is a bittersweet apple cultivar originating in 19th-century England. Known for its early harvest, disease resistance, and use in cider production, it remains cultivated in commercial and traditional orchards.

KWJC, branded as "91.9 Classical KC," is a classical music station licensed to Liberty, Missouri, and broadcasting in the Kansas City metropolitan area on 91.9 MHz. Owned by the University of Missouri-Kansas City (UMKC), the station first went on the air in 1974 under the call sign KWPB, initially serving the William Jewell College campus. Over the decades, KWJC has undergone several format changes, including a stint as "The Edge" playing alternative music and "Jewell 91.9" with an adult contemporary format. In 2019, the station was sold to UMKC and flipped to a full-time classical music format, returning the genre to the FM band in Kansas City.

The Swallow Music Awards (Armenian: Ծիծեռնակ երաժշտական մրցանակաբաշխություն) is an annual music awards ceremony in Armenia, presented by the Ministry of Culture since its inception in 2017. Held at the Armenian National Academic Theatre of Opera and Ballet, the awards recognize excellence and outstanding achievement in various music genres, including classical, folk, and pop. Unlike other Armenian music awards, the Swallow Music Awards features a nomination process based on submitted applications, with specific delegations determining winners in each genre. The awards have faced some controversy, particularly regarding the application requirement, which has drawn criticism from certain artists.
Callulops fojaensis is a rare frog species endemic to the Foja Mountains of Indonesia, known for its robust body and distinctive advertisement call.
Oswald Chettle Mazengarb CBE QC (1890–1963) was a New Zealand barrister, politician, and legal writer. He was a member of the Legislative Council in 1950.

Chenopodium fremontii, also known as fremont goosefoot, is an annual herb growing up to 50 to 80 cm tall with powdery leaves and flowers. It blooms from June to October in spikes of tightly packed tiny flowers. This plant, native to North America, is used by many Native American tribes for both food and medicine, with its greens eaten as vegetables and seeds used for bread and porridge.
The Merchant Street Historic District is a 7-acre (2.8 ha) commercial area in Honolulu, Hawaii, featuring 19th- and early 20th-century architecture.
Nuvilly is a municipality in the Broye district of Fribourg, Switzerland, with a population of 471 as of 2020.

Nnamdi Moweta (born March 27, 1958) is a Nigerian radio host, music promoter, and boxing promoter based in the United States. Originally from Jos Plateau State, Nigeria, he moved to the U.S. in 1978 to study film-making and later established himself as a DJ and music consultant in Los Angeles. Moweta is best known for hosting Radio Afrodicia, which showcases African music and artists, and for promoting events like the African Village Festival. He has also managed notable musicians, served as a music supervisor for award-winning films, and promoted boxing, managing fighters and conducting interviews for various international news programs.

Airblue, founded in 2003 and headquartered in Islamabad, Pakistan, commenced operations on June 18, 2004. Initially serving domestic routes, the airline expanded to international destinations like Dubai and Manchester. It operated a fleet of 12 aircraft and had hubs at Jinnah International Airport in Karachi. Airblue ceased operations in 2014 due to technical issues with its A340 fleet and financial challenges.

Ali Alipour Ghara (born 11 November 1995) is an Iranian professional footballer who plays as a forward for Portuguese club Gil Vicente. He began his career with Nassaji Mazandaran and later played for clubs including Persepolis, where he became the youngest scorer in the Tehran derby and was the Persian Gulf Pro League top scorer in 2017–18. Alipour has also represented the Iran national team, debuting in 2018.

The Puzzle Mansion is a museum and bed-and-breakfast in Tagaytay, Philippines, established in 2012. Founded by Georgina Gil-Lacuna, it housed her extensive jigsaw puzzle collection, which once held a Guinness World Record.

Malice Mizer was a Japanese art rock and gothic rock band active from 1992 to 2001. Formed by Mana and Közi in 1992, the band evolved through different eras, including the Tetsu era (1992–1994), the Gackt era (1995–1999), and the Klaha era (2000–2001). Each era saw distinct changes in their sound and visual style, reflecting their evolving artistic vision. Key albums include "Memoire" (1994), "Voyage ~Sans Retour~" (1996), "Merveilles" (1998), and "Bara no Seidou" (2000). The band's members, including Gackt and Kami, pursued individual careers after Malice Mizer disbanded. Since then, deep sanctuary reunions have occasionally brought the original members back together, with performances marking significant anniversaries. Malice Mizer's music and style continue to influence contemporary artists and remain a cornerstone of the gothic rock and visual kei movements.

Edward A. Keenan (October 15, 1894 – April 12, 1970) was the 34th Mayor of Burlington, Vermont, serving from 1963 to 1965. A Republican, he previously served on the Burlington Board of Aldermen from 1959 to 1963. Keenan was known for advocating a weak-mayor system during his tenure.

The Redan Football Netball Club, nicknamed the Lions, is an Australian rules football and netball club based in Ballarat, Victoria. Established in 1871, it is one of the oldest football clubs in the region and competes in the Ballarat Football League (BFL). The club has a rich history of success, having won 11 senior football premierships, with notable victories in 1946, 1952, and a dominant period in the 2000s, including titles in 2002, 2003, 2006, 2007, 2009, and 2011. The Lions play their senior matches at City Oval, while junior and female football games are held at Western Oval.

Redan has also produced several individual award winners, including multiple Henderson Medal recipients, such as Jarrod Edwards, who won the medal four times between 2006 and 2009, and Nathan Horbury in 2017. The club has also celebrated Tony Lockett Medal winners, with W. Wells achieving the feat in 1952 and 1953, and other players like D. Atkinson and G. Cahir earning the honor in later years. Known for its strong community presence and competitive spirit, Redan continues to be a prominent club in the Ballarat region.

"Spider-Man: India" is a limited comic book series published by Marvel Comics from November 2004 to February 2005, consisting of four issues. The story follows Pavitr Prabhakar, a young boy from a rural village who moves to Mumbai with his aunt and uncle after the death of his parents. Facing bullying at school, Pavitr gains spider-like powers from an ancient yogi to combat a local crime lord, Nalin Oberoi, who is possessed by a demon. As he embraces his new identity as Spider-Man, he learns the importance of responsibility, ultimately defeating Oberoi and restoring peace to Mumbai while beginning a romance with a classmate, Meera Jain.

*Clidastes* is an extinct genus of mosasaur that lived during the Late Cretaceous (Coniacian to Campanian stages), around 85 to 80 million years ago. One of the smallest mosasaurs, it averaged 2 to 4 meters (6.6 to 13.1 ft) in length, with the largest specimens reaching 6.2 meters (20 feet). Its delicate and slim form, along with a strengthened tail, allowed it to chase down fast-moving prey in shallow waters.

The genus is known from marine deposits primarily in the United States. *Clidastes* is classified within the subfamily Mosasaurinae, closely related to *Mosasaurus*. *Clidastes propython* is the best-studied species, and is known from Campanian-age rocks in the United States and Sweden. *Clidastes iguanavus* was the original type species, but was later replaced by *C. propython*. *Clidastes liodontus* is now considered a *nomen dubium*.

Eastwood After Hours: Live at Carnegie Hall is a 1997 live jazz album by actor and director Clint Eastwood and the Carnegie Hall Jazz Band. Recorded on October 17, 1996, at Carnegie Hall in New York City, the concert featured performances of jazz compositions from Eastwood's films, including a medley in which he played piano. The album includes collaborations with notable jazz musicians such as Kenny Barron, Joshua Redman, and Roy Hargrove. Produced by Phil Ramone and Bruce Ricker, the release received positive reviews, with critics praising its celebration of Eastwood's contributions to jazz in cinema. The album was released by Warner Bros. Records on April 29, 1997.

Zar je važno da l' se peva ili pjeva... World Tour by Croatian singer Lepa Brena ran from November 11, 2017, to August 10, 2022, spanning 95 shows across Europe, North America, and Australia. The tour, sponsored by NetTV Plus, included a sold-out concert at Belgrade's Štark Arena and was postponed in 2020 due to the COVID-19 pandemic.

No Gravity is a 3D space shooter developed by realtech VR, originally released in the late 1990s and open-sourced in 2005. Players control a spaceship to complete various missions. The game has been ported to multiple platforms, including PSP and iOS, and has received moderate reviews.

Maria Clara Eimmart was a German astronomer and engraver born in Nuremberg. She trained as her father's apprentice and produced detailed astronomical illustrations, including a series on lunar phases and depictions of comets and solar eclipses. She died in childbirth in 1707.

Republic Township is a civil township in Marquette County, Michigan. As of the 2010 census, it had a population of 1,060 and covers 119.54 square miles (309.6 km²), including part of the Republic community.

The *Cambridge Literary Review* is a British literary journal that publishes poetry, prose, essays, and translations. Founded in Cambridge, England, the journal has published work by J.H. Prynne, John Wilkinson, John Kinsella, Keston Sutherland, Alice Notley, Peter Riley, John James, Geoffrey Hartman, and many others. The journal has also featured essays by Raymond Geuss, Stefan Collini, Rebecca Stott, and Philip Pettit. Several issues have garnered attention, both favorable and unfavorable, in the *Times Literary Supplement*.

David Production Co., Ltd. (株式会社デイヴィッドプロダクション) is a Japanese animation studio founded in September 2007 by Kōji Kajita and Taito Okiura. Based in Nishitōkyō, Tokyo, it became a subsidiary of Fuji TV in 2014.

Khaled Mohamad Takaji (born 18 November 1986) is a Lebanese footballer and former futsal player who plays as a forward for Lebanese Premier League club Bourj. A versatile forward, he has represented Lebanon internationally in both futsal and football, captaining the futsal team and earning accolades such as the 2008 Arab Futsal Championship top scorer and Best Player awards. Takaji has played for several clubs, including Nejmeh, Ansar, and Akhaa Ahli Aley, and participated in multiple AFC Futsal Championships.

The Soviet Union military aircraft designation systems evolved from a pre-war system using functional divisions and government-assigned numbers, to a post-1940 system with letter abbreviations for design offices followed by sequential numbers, often distinguishing fighters by odd numbers and other types by even numbers. Official designations did not include constructors' names, and aircraft had various internal and industry-specific designations.

Convoy HX 156 was a North Atlantic convoy during World War II that sailed from 22 October to 5 November 1941. It is notable for the sinking of the U.S. destroyer *Reuben James* by German submarine *U-552*, marking the first loss of a U.S. warship in the war. Despite attacks by U-boats and a Focke-Wulf Fw 200 Condor, the convoy reached Liverpool with the aid of British and American escorts.

Edith Guerrier (1870–1958) was an American librarian and social reformer known for her work with immigrant communities in Boston. She founded the Saturday Evening Girls reading club, promoting literacy and cultural exchange among girls from diverse backgrounds. Guerrier also established the Paul Revere Pottery Club, enabling members to earn income through crafts. Throughout her career, she advocated for the role of libraries in society and contributed to national initiatives during World War I. After retiring, she continued to volunteer and write, leaving a lasting impact on library services and community engagement.
*Black Noise: Rap Music and Black Culture in Contemporary America* is a 1994 book by Tricia Rose, examining rap music, black culture, and racial politics. It received a 1995 American Book Award.
François Picquet (1708–1781) was a French Sulpician priest and missionary in New France, known for founding Fort de La Présentation and mobilizing Indigenous allies during colonial conflicts.

William Methwold (c. 1590 – 5 March 1653) was an English merchant, linguist, and administrator for the East India Company. He served as president of Surat (1633–1638), where he negotiated the Goa Convention (1635) with the Portuguese. Earlier, he became the first Englishman to visit the Golconda diamond mines, documenting his travels in *Purchas His Pilgrims* (1626). After returning to England, he became deputy governor of the East India Company (1643–1653) and facilitated its merger with the Assada Company (1649). He died at his London home, Hale House.

Kalopanax (*Kalopanax septemlobus*) is a deciduous tree in the family Araliaceae, native to East Asia. Growing up to 30 meters tall, it has spiny stems and large, palmately lobed leaves. Hardy to −40 °C (−40 °F), it is cultivated as an ornamental tree. In Korea, its young shoots are eaten as a blanched dish. It is considered invasive in parts of the U.S.

The POW/MIA flag is a symbol of the United States' commitment to resolving the fates of American prisoners of war (POWs) and those missing in action (MIA). Designed in 1971 by Newt Heisley for the National League of Families, the flag features a silhouette of a POW, a guard tower, and barbed wire, with the words "POW/MIA" and "You Are Not Forgotten." Officially recognized by Congress in 1990, it is flown on federal properties and military sites to honor and remember POW/MIAs from all U.S. conflicts.
Sangita Patel (born January 2, 1979) is a Canadian television personality. She is a co-host on Entertainment Tonight Canada.

Horse pulling originated as a farm challenge and has evolved into an organized sport featuring teams of equine athletes. The sport uses specially designed dynamometers, like the Hensley-Humbert and Midwest models, to test and measure the pulling power of horses. Competitions begin with a starting load of 1500 pounds, which teams must pull for a short distance. Only successful teams advance to subsequent rounds. Safety regulations, enforced by organizations like the Eastern Draft Horse Association, ensure the well-being of the horses during these events.

Jackson Township is one of 16 townships in Richland County, Ohio, United States. As of the 2000 census, it had a population of 3,861. The township is governed by a three-member board of trustees and includes part of the city of Shelby.

Charles Parker (1809–1898) was a New Zealand politician who served as a Member of the New Zealand Parliament for Motueka and Massacre Bay from 1855 to 1875. Born in England, he emigrated to New Zealand in 1849 and became involved in local politics and business.

The 4th Electromagnetic Warfare Squadron, active since 1990 and part of the United States Space Force, specializes in offensive counterspace operations and space situational awareness. Based at Peterson Space Force Base, Colorado, it supports global military campaigns.

Shigeichi Nagano (1925–2019) was a Japanese photographer known for his street photography, particularly his humorous depictions of Tokyo's salarymen during Japan's economic boom of the 1960s. He also worked in film and television after working on Tokyo Olympiad.

Hank Foldberg (March 12, 1923 – March 7, 2001) was an American football player and coach. Born in Dallas, Texas, he played college football for Texas A&M and the Army Cadets, earning a first-team All-American selection in 1946. Foldberg coached at Purdue, Texas A&M, and the University of Wichita, compiling a 22–28–1 head coaching record. He also served as the athletic director at Texas A&M. After a brief real estate career in Arkansas, he died at age 77.

Siegfried Müller is a German former sidecarcross racer. With driver Eugen Siegle, he finished sixth in the 1974 FIM Cup and fifth in the 1976 FIM European Championship. Partnering with Reinhard Böhler, Müller won the inaugural 1980 FIM World Championship.

"Que viva la lucha" is a 2007 Mexican documentary film directed by Gustavo Vazquez, focusing on the world of lucha libre in Tijuana. Spanning two years, the film follows emerging wrestlers like Extreme Tiger, Angel Negro Jr., and Pancho Cachondo, capturing their dual lives as working-class individuals and local heroes. It explores the cultural significance of lucha libre, highlighting the themes of good versus evil and the transformation of wrestlers through their masks. The documentary also examines the roles of rudos and técnicos, women wrestlers, and the enthusiastic fan base, showcasing the diverse and passionate nature of lucha libre in Tijuana.
St Andrew's Church, Buckland Monachorum, is a Grade I listed medieval church in Devon, England, known for its late 15th-century architecture and notable monuments.

Thrilling is a private vintage clothing company founded in 2018 by Shilla Kim-Parker and Brad Mallow, headquartered in Los Angeles, California. The company operates an online platform that connects brick-and-mortar thrift and vintage stores with customers, distinguishing itself from competitors like Etsy, Depop, Poshmark, and Tradesy, which primarily rely on peer-to-peer sales. Thrilling handles sales, shipping, and returns for a 10% commission, offering technology to help store owners digitize large inventories, including tools for size and price detection. During the early months of the COVID-19 pandemic, the company waived commissions to support shop owners.

Thrilling has raised significant funding since its inception, including $100,000 in seed funding in 2018, $2.1 million in 2019, and $8.5 million in a 2021 Series A round led by Prelude Ventures. Other investors include Elemental Excelerator, Defy, Urban US, Phoenix Rising, Closed Loop, and Congruent Ventures. Despite its innovative business model, Thrilling's social media presence and customer review system remain less developed compared to its competitors. The company continues to focus on supporting small businesses in the vintage and thrift industry.

Occupy San Francisco was a local manifestation of the broader Occupy movement, occurring from September 17 to December 11, 2011. Sparked by issues of economic inequality and corporate influence over government, the movement began with a small group of protesters at a Bank of America branch and expanded to various locations, including the Federal Reserve and Justin Herman Plaza. The encampments faced multiple police raids, resulting in numerous arrests. Notable events included protests against tuition hikes by the University of California Regents and ongoing community support initiatives, such as plans to establish a credit union.

Jamil Baroody (1905–1975) was a Melkite Greek Catholic diplomat who served as Saudi Arabia's representative to the United Nations, shaping Saudi positions and known for his oratorical skills and eccentric speeches. He championed Palestinian statehood and opposed the Universal Declaration of Human Rights, fearing it would threaten Islamic law and Saudi practices.

Marie Woodson is a Haitian-American politician and member of the Florida House of Representatives, representing the 101st district since November 3, 2020. A Democrat, she previously worked as a public administrator for Miami-Dade County.

The men's hammer throw at the 2004 Summer Olympics took place from August 20 to 22 at the Olympic Stadium, featuring 35 competitors from 24 nations. Initially, Adrián Annus of Hungary won the event but was disqualified for doping, leading to Koji Murofushi of Japan being awarded the gold medal. Ivan Tsikhan of Belarus, who originally won silver, was also disqualified in 2012 for a doping violation, resulting in Eşref Apak of Turkey receiving the bronze medal. This event marked the 24th occurrence of the men's hammer throw in Olympic history.

Whitland Abbey, founded on 16 September 1140 by monks from Clairvaux Abbey, became a significant religious and cultural center in southwest Wales. It played a crucial role in supporting the Welsh struggle for independence and was under the patronage of Rhys ap Gruffudd, Prince of Deheubarth. The abbey, dedicated to St. Mary, flourished until the dissolution under Henry VIII, with revenues estimated at £153.17.2. A forge later established on the site and a Victorian house were owned by the Yelverton and Blake families. The abbey's historical significance includes its involvement in the defense of Cynvael Castle and the final days of Maredudd, Rhys's son, as a monk.

Edward North was a British Conservative politician who served as the Member of Parliament for Nuneaton from 1931 to 1935. Born in 1892, he was educated at Eton and Trinity College, Cambridge. North also had a military career, serving as a Major in the Royal Armoured Corps during World War II. He passed away on January 1, 1942.

Ahmad Nami (1873 – December 13, 1962) was a Syrian politician who served as the 5th Prime Minister and 2nd President of Syria from 1926 to 1928. Born in Beirut to an affluent family, he was of Turkish and Circassian descent and had military training in Paris. Nami advocated for Syrian unity and sought to establish a national army, but his tenure ended amid political challenges and French opposition. After retiring from public life, he occasionally lectured at the Sorbonne.

Sir Robert Buckland KBE KC MP (born 22 September 1968) served as Secretary of State for Wales from 7 July 2022 to 25 October 2022. He previously held the positions of Secretary of State for Justice and Lord Chancellor from 24 July 2019 to 15 September 2021, and Minister of State for Prisons from 9 May 2019 to 24 July 2019. Buckland, a member of the Conservative Party, has been the Member of Parliament for South Swindon since 2010. He has held various roles in the government, including Solicitor General for England and Wales and a member of several parliamentary committees. Buckland has received numerous awards for his advocacy work, including the Politician of the Year Award from the Royal College of Speech and Language Therapists and the Grassroot Diplomat Initiative Award.

"All Good Things..." is the series finale of the American reality television series *The Hills*, serving as the 12th episode of its sixth season. It originally aired on MTV on July 13, 2010, and was narrated by Kristin Cavallari. The episode follows the main cast as they prepare for significant life changes, including Kristin's decision to move to Europe, Audrina purchasing a new home, and Lo moving in with her boyfriend. The finale concludes with a memorable twist: as Kristin departs in a limousine, the Hollywood Hills backdrop is revealed to be a set, hinting at the show's scripted nature. An alternate ending, aired in 2013, features Lauren Conrad returning to comfort Brody Jenner after Kristin's departure.

The episode received mixed reviews, with critics divided on its self-referential ending and recurring themes of moving on. While some praised the finale's clever twist, others criticized it for exploiting long-standing allegations of scripting. The alternate ending also garnered varied reactions, with some appreciating Conrad's return and others preferring the original conclusion. "All Good Things..." was watched by 3 million viewers during its initial broadcast, making it the most-watched episode of the season. The sixth season was later released on DVD in September 2010.
The Taylorcraft Auster is a British light aircraft developed from the American Taylorcraft Model A, primarily used as an Air Observation Post (AOP) during World War II.

Valmiera is a city in Latvia, founded in 1323 and located in the Valmiera municipality. Situated at 57°33′N 25°24′E, it covers an area of 19.36 km² with a population of 22,376 (as of 2024). Valmiera has a rich history, with archaeological evidence indicating human habitation dating back 9,000 years. The city played a significant role in the Hanseatic League and experienced periods of devastation and reconstruction, particularly during the Great Northern War and World War II. Today, Valmiera is an important industrial center, known for its food industry, fibreglass production, and wood processing. The city is also recognized for its strong educational infrastructure and vibrant sports culture, hosting numerous sports organizations and events. Valmiera is twinned with several sister cities.
Ruth Cameron is a vocalist and album co-producer.

George Ege (March 9, 1748 – December 14, 1829) was an American ironmaster, politician, and Federalist who served in the U.S. House of Representatives from Pennsylvania's 5th district (1796–1797) and in the Pennsylvania House of Representatives (1783). Born in Germantown, Pennsylvania, he became a prominent ironmaster, owning the Charming Forge and other properties, and was one of Berks County's wealthiest citizens. Ege also served as an associate judge in Berks County from 1791 to 1818.
The 2022–23 season was AS Saint-Étienne's first in Ligue 2 following relegation from Ligue 1. Led by head coach Laurent Batlles, the team competed in Ligue 2 and the Coupe de France.

Burlington Street is an 8.5-kilometre (5.3 mi) arterial road in Hamilton, Ontario, running along the city's industrial waterfront. Originally named Industrial Road, it was renamed for its proximity to Hamilton Harbour (historically called Burlington Bay). The street became a major industrial corridor in the mid-20th century, hosting companies like Stelco, Dofasco, Procter & Gamble, and International Harvester. Today, it remains a key route for Hamilton's port operations, steel production, and manufacturing, including facilities like Lakeport Brewing Company and Bunge Canada. The road also features Nikola Tesla Boulevard, an elevated expressway section honoring the inventor whose alternating current technology powered Hamilton's early industrial growth. Burlington Street continues to play a vital role in the city's economy and transportation network.

The slender-billed gull (Chroicocephalus genei) is a medium-sized gull, measuring 37 to 40 cm in length, with a wingspan of 90 to 102 cm. It is characterized by its pale grey body, white head, and elongated dark red bill. This species breeds in lagoons and lakes around the Mediterranean and the northwestern Indian Ocean, and it overwinters along coastal estuaries and bays. The slender-billed gull primarily feeds on fish and marine invertebrates, and it is classified as Least Concern by the IUCN.

Khan Bahadur Nassakh (Abū Muḥammad ʿAbdul Ghafūr, 1833 – 1889) was a Bengali Muslim poet and government officer known for his contributions to Urdu and Persian literature. Born into the Qadi family in Faridpur, he served as a deputy magistrate and collector in the Bengal Presidency. Notable works include *Sukhan-e-Shuara*, *Daftar-e-Bemisal*, and *Tazkiratul Muasirin*, with his poetry praised by contemporaries like Ghalib.

Richard Stöhr (born Richard Stern; 1874 – December 1967) was an Austrian composer and music theorist, known for his contributions to classical music and education. Born in Vienna to Jewish parents, he began composing at the age of six and later studied at the Vienna Academy of Music under Robert Fuchs, where he also became a professor. Stöhr's career flourished in Austria until the rise of the Nazi regime, which led to his dismissal from the Academy due to his Jewish heritage. In 1939, he emigrated to the United States, where he taught at the Curtis Institute of Music and later at Saint Michael's College in Vermont. Throughout his life, Stöhr composed a wide range of works, including symphonies, chamber music, and choral pieces, though many of his later compositions remained unpublished. He passed away in 1967 and is remembered for his adherence to the tonal traditions of the 19th century amidst the evolving musical landscape.

Władysław Studnicki (1867–1953) was a Polish politician, publicist, and political activist. Initially a socialist, he later embraced nationalism and advocated for Polish-German cooperation. He envisioned a Central European bloc against the Soviet Union. In 1939, he opposed the alliance with Britain, warning of dire consequences for Poland. During World War II, he attempted to influence German policy and was imprisoned. After the war, he lived in exile, defending his controversial views.

"The Lady in the Lake" is a 1943 detective novel by Raymond Chandler featuring the private investigator Philip Marlowe. The plot follows Marlowe as he is hired by wealthy businessman Derace Kingsley to locate his estranged wife, Crystal, who has gone missing. As Marlowe investigates, he uncovers a series of murders and a complex web of deception involving multiple identities. The novel is noted for its intricate narrative and exploration of themes such as ambiguity and identity, and it has been praised as one of Chandler's masterpieces.

CNN Pipeline was an internet streaming television network launched by Time Warner on December 5, 2005, and operational until June 27, 2007. It replaced portions of CNN Interactive, offering subscribers access to live news feeds and video archives. The service featured four simultaneous feeds, known as "pipes," and was anchored by Veronica De La Cruz, Nicole Lapin, Melissa Long, and Richard Lui. CNN Pipeline was discontinued to transition to a free, ad-supported model, leading to the launch of CNNgo in 2014, which incorporated many of Pipeline's features.

Lee Franklin Hanmer (October 24, 1871 – April 27, 1961) was an American educator and recreation advocate. He worked in New York City schools, led the Playground Association of America, and directed wartime recreation programs during World War I. He received the Boy Scouts of America's Silver Buffalo Award in 1926.

Rui Pedro Gonçalves Pinto, born on October 20, 1988, in Vila Nova de Gaia, Portugal, is a former football data journalist and whistleblower. Active from 2015 to 2019, Pinto was involved in the Football Leaks scandal, where he exposed internal emails from Doyen Sports Investments, leading to international investigations and revelations about tax evasion in the football industry. He was arrested in Budapest in January 2019 on charges of attempted qualified extortion, violation of secrecy, and illegal access to information. Pinto's case, which garnered attention and support, including winning the European Whistleblower Award in 2019, remains on trial as of 2020.
"The Time" is a studio album by Chinese artist Xu Weizhou, released between 2017 and 2018, featuring a mix of C-pop, rock, and EDM.

Thomas P. Coates (1803 – October 11, 1895) was an American band conductor and composer. He played the cornet and French horn. Coates conducted the regimental band of the 47th Pennsylvania Volunteer Infantry during the American Civil War. His "Twelfth Funeral March" was played at Ulysses S. Grant's funeral. Coates has been nicknamed the "Father of Band Music in America."
Judith Révész (1915–2018) was a Hungarian-Dutch potter and sculptor, known for her earthenware and small ceramic figures.

Qin Shengxiang (born February 1957 in Jianli, Hubei, China) served as the Political Commissar of the People's Liberation Army Navy from September 2017 to January 2022, and Director of the General Office of the Central Military Commission from October 2012 to August 2017. He held the rank of admiral and was a member of the Communist Party of China.

Multiple complex developmental disorder (MCDD) is a neurological and psychological condition characterized by a combination of symptoms that do not fit neatly into specific diagnostic categories. The diagnostic criteria for MCDD are debated and are not included in the DSM-V or ICD-10, leading to ambiguity in its definition. Symptoms may include psychotic, affective, behavioral, autistic, and neurological features, often influenced by various genetic factors. Each individual with MCDD presents a unique profile of symptoms, complicating diagnosis and treatment.

Velocity Tower is a residential building located in Sheffield, South Yorkshire. Construction began in 2006, and the building opened to tenants in December 2008, though unfinished. The tower currently stands at 22 of the planned 30 stories.
Sangita Patel (born January 2, 1979) is a Canadian television personality, known for her work on *Entertainment Tonight Canada*, *CP24*, and *The Weather Network*.

Joanna Mytkowska, born in 1970, is a Polish curator, art critic, and museum director. She studied at the University of Warsaw and has curated exhibitions at the Centre Georges Pompidou and the Polish Pavilion at the Venice Biennale. Mytkowska was a member of the selection committee for Documenta 14 and received the Igor Zabel Award in 2018.

Lewis Madison Terman (January 15, 1877 – December 21, 1956) was an American psychologist and professor at Stanford University, best known for his contributions to intelligence testing. He adapted the Binet-Simon Scale into the Stanford-Binet Intelligence Scales, popularizing the intelligence quotient (IQ). Terman conducted the Genetic Studies of Genius, a longitudinal study of gifted children, challenging stereotypes about intelligence and success. He also played a key role in World War I military testing and supported eugenics, advocating for intelligence-based social policies. His work influenced educational psychology, though his views on race and heredity remain controversial. His son, Frederick Terman, became a prominent engineer and academic.

Advance America is a conservative Christian advocacy group founded in 1980 by Eric Miller in Indiana. It publishes voter guides, informs people about legislation, and lobbies the Indiana state legislature on issues it considers to affect the family and religious freedom.

Fight Sports is a Brazilian jiu-jitsu academy founded in 2011 by six-time World No-Gi and ADCC champion Roberto "Cyborg" Abreu in Miami, USA. The organization has expanded globally, with over thirty affiliate schools across four continents as of July 2022. Fight Sports has faced controversy, notably involving allegations against a prominent coach in 2018.

Valmiera is a state city in Latvia, located on the Gauja River. With a population of 22,376 as of 2024, it serves as the center of Valmiera Municipality. First mentioned as a town in 1323, Valmiera was a member of the Hanseatic League from the 14th–16th centuries, fostering trade and development. The city has a rich history, experiencing periods of devastation and growth, and has been an important industrial, educational, and sports center in Latvia.

Koforidua Senior High Technical School (KOSTECH) is a co-educational secondary technical school in Koforidua, Ghana, established in 1967 by Dr. Kwame Nkrumah. Originally an all-boys institution, it began admitting girls in 1991. The school offers General Science and Technical programs and is ranked among Ghana's top Class A schools. It gained recognition for outstanding WASSCE results, including a record 200 A+ grades in 2011.

Kafiristan, meaning "land of the infidels" in Persian, is a historical region located in present-day northeastern Afghanistan and northwestern Pakistan. The name derives from the Arabic term *kāfir*, meaning "non-believer," and historically referred to the region's inhabitants, who practiced a form of polytheism before their forced conversion to Islam in 1895–96 under Emir Abdur Rahman Khan of Afghanistan. Following their conversion, the region was renamed Nuristan, meaning "land of the enlightened."

Kafiristan was historically part of the ancient Kapisa kingdom, which flourished under Buddhist rule until the decline of Buddhism in the region. The area became isolated and retained its distinct culture and religion until the late 19th century. European explorers, including Bento de Góis, Alexander Gardner, and George Scott Robertson, documented the region and its people before their conversion.

The region was known for its steep valleys, intricate wood carvings, and unique cultural practices. After its conquest, many artifacts were destroyed or repurposed, with some preserved in museums. A small group of Kafirs fled to Chitral, where the Kalasha people, descendants of the "Black Kafirs," continue to practice their traditional beliefs. Today, the region is part of Afghanistan's Nuristan Province and Pakistan's Chitral District, with the Kalasha culture surviving in isolated valleys.
John's River, or Abhainn Naomh Eoin in Irish, is a 6-kilometer river in Waterford, Ireland, flowing into the River Suir and characterized by its picturesque course through the city.

Wawne is a village and civil parish in the East Riding of Yorkshire, England, located about 5 miles north of Hull. First recorded in the Domesday Book (1086) as *Wagene*, its name likely derives from Old English meaning "quagmire." Historically part of a large medieval parish, Wawne once included Sutton-on-Hull and Stoneferry. The village features St Peter's Church, a Grade I listed building, and was historically served by a ferry across the River Hull until 1946. As of the 2011 census, it had a population of 975.

Wild Things: Foursome is a 2010 American erotic thriller film directed by Andy Hurst. A direct-to-video sequel to *Wild Things* (1998), it follows a group of conspirators—including a wealthy heir (Jillian Murray) and a scheming mechanic (Marnette Patterson)—who plot to inherit a fortune through murder and deception. The film received negative reviews for its derivative plot and lack of originality.
Thierry Escaich is a French composer and organist known for his diverse works.

Cuneo railway station, located in Piazzale della Libertà, Cuneo, Piedmont, Italy, is a key transportation hub that opened on November 7, 1937. Operated by Rete Ferroviaria Italiana and classified as a Gold station, it serves multiple lines, including Fossano–Cuneo and Cuneo–Ventimiglia. The station features five passenger tracks and eight additional tracks for goods traffic, along with a locomotive depot. With approximately 3 million passenger movements annually, it provides regional train services to destinations such as Turin and Savona. Since March 30, 2009, the station has implemented a modern public information system to enhance communication and service efficiency.

Eclipse Island is a granite island located off the south coast of Western Australia, covering 99 hectares (245 acres). Named by Captain George Vancouver in 1791 after observing a solar eclipse, it is a Class 1A Nature Reserve and an Important Bird Area, supporting significant populations of seabirds. The island features a lighthouse, weather station, and a historic landing stage.

Beta Delphini, also known as Rotanev, is a binary star system in the constellation Delphinus. Consisting of an F-type giant star (Beta Delphini A) and a subgiant star (Beta Delphini B), it orbits every 26.66 years. The system, discovered in 1873, is approximately 1.8 billion years old, with Beta Delphini A having 1.75 solar masses and 24 times the Sun's luminosity, while Beta Delphini B has 1.47 solar masses and 8 times the Sun's luminosity.

Baankey Ki Crazy Baraat, directed by Aijaz Khan and starring Rajpal Yadav, follows a 35-year-old bachelor who tricks his family into arranging a proxy wedding for him. The film, released in 2015, features a quirky plot with unexpected twists and turns, ultimately resulting in the groom-to-be and bride-to-be falling in love. While praised for its performances and technical aspects, the film received mixed reviews from critics.

Give Notice (18 May 1997 – ?) was a British Thoroughbred racehorse best known for winning the Group 1 Prix du Cadran (2002) and the Group 3 Sagaro Stakes (2002). Trained by John Dunlop and owned by Ian Stewart-Brown and Michael Meacock, he was a durable stayer who competed in long-distance races, including the Ascot Gold Cup and Goodwood Cup. A bay gelding by Warning, he earned £185,219 in prize money over 27 starts, winning seven races. His dam, Princess Genista, also produced Times Up, a dual Doncaster Cup winner.

The Oregon State University Foundation, formed on October 15, 1947, partners with the university to enhance its excellence and impact through resource stewardship. With a rich history and significant growth in its endowment, the foundation has played a crucial role in major fundraising campaigns, including The Campaign for OSU and Believe It: The Campaign for Oregon State University.
